**Belfast** ([/ˈbɛlfæst/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q1860_(eng)-Vealhurl-Belfast.wav "File:LL-Q1860 (eng)-Vealhurl-Belfast.wav"), [*BEL-fast*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"), [/-fɑːst/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"), [*-⁠fahst*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key");[[a]](#cite_note-3) from [Irish](/wiki/Irish_language "Irish language"): *Béal Feirste* [[bʲeːlˠ ˈfʲɛɾˠ(ə)ʃtʲə]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Irish "Help:IPA/Irish")[ⓘ](/wiki/File:Uladh_-_Aontroim_-_B%C3%A9al_Feirste.wav "File:Uladh - Aontroim - Béal Feirste.wav"))[[3]](#cite_note-4)[[4]](#cite_note-Logainm-5) is the capital city and principal port of [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland"), standing on the banks of the [River Lagan](/wiki/River_Lagan "River Lagan") and connected to the open sea through [Belfast Lough](/wiki/Belfast_Lough "Belfast Lough") and the [North Channel](/wiki/North_Channel_(Great_Britain_and_Ireland) "North Channel (Great Britain and Ireland)"). It is the largest city in Northern Ireland and the second-largest city in [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland") (after [Dublin](/wiki/Dublin "Dublin")), with an estimated population of 352,390 in 2024,[[2]](#cite_note-popstats-2) and [its metropolitan area](/wiki/Belfast_metropolitan_area "Belfast metropolitan area") has a population of 671,559.[[5]](#cite_note-:17-6)

While chartered as an English settlement in 1613, the town's early growth was driven by an influx of [Scottish](/wiki/Scottish_people "Scottish people") [Presbyterians](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_in_Ireland "Presbyterian Church in Ireland"). Their descendants' disaffection with [Ireland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ireland "Kingdom of Ireland")'s [Anglican establishment](/wiki/Protestant_Ascendancy "Protestant Ascendancy") contributed to the [rebellion of 1798](/wiki/Irish_Rebellion_of_1798 "Irish Rebellion of 1798"), and to the [union](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1800 "Acts of Union 1800") with [Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain") that followed in 1801—later regarded as a key to the town's industrial transformation. When granted [city status](/wiki/City_status_in_the_United_Kingdom#Northern_Ireland "City status in the United Kingdom") in 1888, Belfast was the world's largest centre of [linen](/wiki/Linen "Linen") manufacture, and by the 1900s its shipyards were building up to a quarter of total [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland") tonnage.

[Sectarian](/wiki/Sectarianism "Sectarianism") tensions existed with the [Irish Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_Ireland "Catholic Church in Ireland") population that was drawn by mill and factory employment from western counties. Heightened by division over Ireland's future in the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland"), these twice erupted in periods of sustained violence: [in 1920–22](/wiki/The_Troubles_in_Ulster_(1920%E2%80%931922) "The Troubles in Ulster (1920–1922)"), as Belfast emerged as the capital of the [six northeast counties](/wiki/Partition_of_Ireland "Partition of Ireland") retaining the British connection, and over [three decades from the late 1960s](/wiki/The_Troubles "The Troubles") during which the [British Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army") was continually deployed on the streets. A legacy of conflict is the [barrier-reinforced separation](/wiki/Peace_lines "Peace lines") of [Protestant](/wiki/Protestantism_in_Ireland "Protestantism in Ireland") and Catholic [working-class](/wiki/Working_class "Working class") districts.

Since the [Good Friday Agreement](/wiki/1998_Northern_Ireland_Good_Friday_Agreement_referendum "1998 Northern Ireland Good Friday Agreement referendum"), the electoral balance in the once [unionist](/wiki/Unionism_in_Ireland "Unionism in Ireland")-controlled city has shifted, albeit with no overall majority, in favour of [Irish nationalists](/wiki/Irish_nationalism "Irish nationalism"). At the same time, new immigrants are adding to the growing number of residents unwilling to identify with either of the two communal traditions.

Belfast has seen significant [services sector](/wiki/Tertiary_sector_of_the_economy "Tertiary sector of the economy") growth, with important contributions from financial technology ([fintech](/wiki/Fintech "Fintech")), tourism and, with facilities in the redeveloped [Harbour Estate](/wiki/Belfast_Harbour "Belfast Harbour"), film. It retains a port with commercial and industrial docks, including a reduced [Harland & Wolff](/wiki/Harland_%26_Wolff "Harland & Wolff") shipyard and aerospace and defence contractors. Under the [Windsor Framework](/wiki/Windsor_Framework "Windsor Framework"), Belfast and Northern Ireland remain within the United Kingdom’s internal market while continuing to apply aspects of the [EU single market](/wiki/European_single_market "European single market") rules for goods.

The city is served by two airports: [George Best Belfast City Airport](/wiki/George_Best_Belfast_City_Airport "George Best Belfast City Airport"), located on the Lough shore, and [Belfast International Airport](/wiki/Belfast_International_Airport "Belfast International Airport") (also known as Aldergrove), located 15 miles (24 kilometres) west of the city. Belfast supports two universities: [Ulster University](/wiki/Ulster_University "Ulster University") in the city centre, and in the Botanic area in the south of the city, [Queen's University Belfast](/wiki/Queen%27s_University_Belfast "Queen's University Belfast"). Since 2021, Belfast has been a [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") designated [City of Music](/wiki/City_of_Music_(UNESCO) "City of Music (UNESCO)").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Belfast](/wiki/History_of_Belfast "History of Belfast")

### Name

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Name")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Ground_Plan_of_Belfast_%281685%29.png/250px-Ground_Plan_of_Belfast_%281685%29.png)](/wiki/File:Ground_Plan_of_Belfast_(1685).png)

A 1685 plan of Belfast by the military engineer [Thomas Phillips](/wiki/Thomas_Phillips_(engineer) "Thomas Phillips (engineer)"), showing the town's ramparts and [Lord Chichester's castle](/wiki/Belfast_Castle#Plantation_Castle "Belfast Castle"), which was destroyed in a fire in 1708

The name Belfast derives from the Irish *Béal Feirste* (Irish pronunciation: [[bʲeːlˠ ˈfʲɛɾˠ(ə)ʃtʲə]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Irish "Help:IPA/Irish")),[[4]](#cite_note-Logainm-5) "Mouth of the [Farset](/wiki/River_Farset "River Farset")",[[6]](#cite_note-7) a river whose name in the Irish, *Feirste,* refers to a sandbar or tidal ford.[[7]](#cite_note-Belfast_name-8) This was formed where the river ran—until culverted late in the 18th century, down High Street—[[8]](#cite_note-townbook-1892-9) into the Lagan. It was at this crossing, located under or close to the current Queen's Bridge, that the early settlement developed.[[9]](#cite_note-:22-10): 74–77

The compilers of [Ulster-Scots](/wiki/Ulster_Scots_dialects "Ulster Scots dialects") use various transcriptions of local pronunciations of "Belfast" (with which they sometimes are also content)[[10]](#cite_note-11) including *Bilfawst*,[[11]](#cite_note-12)[[12]](#cite_note-13) *Bilfaust*[[13]](#cite_note-14) or *Baelfawst.*[[14]](#cite_note-15)

As a legacy of emigration, Belfast has lent its name to more than a dozen settlements in the United States, of which the largest is [Belfast, Maine](/wiki/Belfast,_Maine "Belfast, Maine"), and to one each [in Australia](/wiki/Belfast,_Victoria "Belfast, Victoria"), [Canada](/wiki/Belfast,_Prince_Edward_Island "Belfast, Prince Edward Island"), [New Zealand](/wiki/Belfast,_New_Zealand "Belfast, New Zealand") and [South Africa](/wiki/Belfast,_Mpumalanga "Belfast, Mpumalanga").

### Early settlements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early settlements")]

The site of Belfast has been occupied since the [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age"). The [Giant's Ring](/wiki/Giants_Ring,_Belfast "Giants Ring, Belfast"), a 5,000-year-old [henge](/wiki/Henge "Henge"), is located near the city,[[9]](#cite_note-:22-10): 42–45[[15]](#cite_note-16) and the remains of [Iron Age](/wiki/Iron_Age "Iron Age") [hill forts](/wiki/Hill_fort "Hill fort") can still be seen in the surrounding hills. At the beginning of the 14th century, [Papal](/wiki/Papacy "Papacy") tax rolls record two churches: the "Chapel of Dundela" at Knock (Irish: *[cnoc](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/cnoc "wikt:cnoc")*, meaning "hill") in the east,[[16]](#cite_note-17) connected by some accounts to the 7th-century evangelist [St. Colmcille](/wiki/Columba "Columba"),[[17]](#cite_note-:21-18): 11and, the "Chapel of the Ford", which may have been a successor to a much older parish church on the present [Shankill *(Seanchill*, "Old Church") Road](/wiki/Shankill_Road "Shankill Road"),[[9]](#cite_note-:22-10): 63–64 dating back to the 9th,[[18]](#cite_note-19) and possibly to [St. Patrick](/wiki/Saint_Patrick "Saint Patrick") in the mid 5th, century.[[19]](#cite_note-20)

A [Norman](/wiki/Normans_in_Ireland "Normans in Ireland") settlement at the ford, comprising the parish church (now [St. George's](/wiki/St_George%27s_Church,_Belfast "St George's Church, Belfast")), a watermill, and a small fort,[[20]](#cite_note-:162-21) was an outpost of [Carrickfergus Castle](/wiki/Carrickfergus_Castle "Carrickfergus Castle"). Established in the late 12th century, 11 miles (18 km) out along the north shore of the Lough, Carrickfergus was to remain the principal English foothold in the north-east until the [scorched- earth](/wiki/Scorched_earth "Scorched earth") [Nine Years' War](/wiki/Nine_Years%27_War_(Ireland) "Nine Years' War (Ireland)") at the end of the 16th century broke the remaining Irish power, the [O'Neills](/wiki/O%27Neill_dynasty "O'Neill dynasty").[[21]](#cite_note-:202-22)

### Developing port, radical politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Developing port, radical politics")]

With a commission from [King James VI and I](/wiki/James_VI_and_I "James VI and I"), in 1613 [Sir Arthur Chichester](/wiki/Arthur_Chichester,_1st_Baron_Chichester "Arthur Chichester, 1st Baron Chichester") undertook the [Plantation](/wiki/Plantations_of_Ireland "Plantations of Ireland") of Belfast and the surrounding area, attracting mainly English and [Manx](/wiki/Isle_of_Man "Isle of Man") settlers.[[22]](#cite_note-23) The subsequent arrival of [Scottish Presbyterians](/wiki/Ulster_Scots_people "Ulster Scots people") embroiled Belfast in its only recorded siege: denounced from London by [John Milton](/wiki/John_Milton "John Milton") as "ungrateful and treacherous guests",[[23]](#cite_note-24) in 1649 the newcomers were temporarily expelled by an English [Parliamentarian](/wiki/Roundhead "Roundhead") army.[[24]](#cite_note-:16-25): 21[[25]](#cite_note-:26-26): 32 In 1689, Catholic [Jacobite](/wiki/Jacobitism "Jacobitism") forces, briefly in command of the town,[[26]](#cite_note-27) abandoned it in advance of the landing at Carrickfergus of [William, Prince of Orange](/wiki/William_III_of_England "William III of England"), who proceeded through Belfast to his celebrated victory on 12 July 1690 at [the Boyne](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Boyne "Battle of the Boyne").[[27]](#cite_note-28)

Together with French [Huguenots](/wiki/Huguenots "Huguenots"), the Scots introduced the production of [linen](/wiki/Linen "Linen"), a [flax](/wiki/Flax "Flax")-spinning industry that in the 18th century carried Belfast trade to the Americas.[[28]](#cite_note-29) Fortunes were made carrying rough linen clothing and salted provisions to the [slave plantations](/wiki/Slave_plantation "Slave plantation") of the [West Indies](/wiki/West_Indies "West Indies"); sugar and rum to [Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore "Baltimore") and New York City; and for the return to Belfast [flaxseed](/wiki/Flax "Flax") and tobacco from [the colonies](/wiki/Thirteen_Colonies "Thirteen Colonies").[[29]](#cite_note-30) From the 1760s, profits from the trade financed improvements in the town's commercial infrastructure, including the [Lagan Canal](/wiki/Lagan_Canal "Lagan Canal"), new docks and quays, and the construction of the White Linen Hall which together attracted to Belfast the linen trade that had formerly gone through [Dublin](/wiki/Dublin "Dublin"). [Abolitionist](/wiki/Abolitionism "Abolitionism") sentiment, however, defeated the proposal of the greatest of the merchant houses, [Cunningham and Greg](/wiki/Waddell_Cunningham "Waddell Cunningham"), in 1786 to commission ships for the [Middle Passage](/wiki/Middle_Passage "Middle Passage").[[30]](#cite_note-Rodgers-31)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Bastille-day-belfast-1791.jpg/250px-Bastille-day-belfast-1791.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bastille-day-belfast-1791.jpg)

Volunteer Corps parade down High Street, [Bastille Day](/wiki/Bastille_Day "Bastille Day"), 1792

As "Dissenters" from the [established Anglican church](/wiki/Church_of_Ireland "Church of Ireland") (with its [episcopacy](/wiki/Episcopacy "Episcopacy") and ritual), [Presbyterians](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_in_Ireland "Presbyterian Church in Ireland") were conscious of sharing, if only in part, the [disabilities](/wiki/Penal_Laws_against_Irish_Catholics "Penal Laws against Irish Catholics") of Ireland's dispossessed [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_Ireland "Catholic Church in Ireland") majority; and of being denied representation in the [Irish Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Ireland "Parliament of Ireland"). Belfast's two [MPs](/wiki/Member_of_parliament "Member of parliament") remained nominees of the Chichesters ([Marquesses of Donegall](/wiki/Marquess_of_Donegall "Marquess of Donegall")). With their emigrant kinsmen in America, the region's Presbyterians were to share a growing disaffection from [the Crown.](/wiki/The_British_Crown "The British Crown")[[31]](#cite_note-:3-32): 55–61[[32]](#cite_note-33)

When early in the [American War of Independence](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"), [Belfast Lough was raided](/wiki/North_Channel_Naval_Duel "North Channel Naval Duel") by the [privateer](/wiki/Privateer "Privateer") [John Paul Jones](/wiki/John_Paul_Jones "John Paul Jones"), the townspeople assembled their own [Volunteer militia](/wiki/Irish_Volunteers_(18th_century) "Irish Volunteers (18th century)"). Formed ostensibly for defence of [the Kingdom](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ireland "Kingdom of Ireland"), Volunteer corps were soon pressing their own protest against "taxation without representation". Further emboldened by the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution"), a more radical element in the town, the [Society of United Irishmen](/wiki/Society_of_United_Irishmen "Society of United Irishmen"), called for [Catholic emancipation](/wiki/Catholic_emancipation "Catholic emancipation") and a representative national government.[[33]](#cite_note-Connolly-34) In hopes of French assistance, in 1798 the Society organised a republican insurrection. The rebel tradesmen and tenant farmers were defeated north of the town at the [Battle of Antrim](/wiki/Battle_of_Antrim "Battle of Antrim") and to the south at the [Battle of Ballynahinch](/wiki/Battle_of_Ballynahinch "Battle of Ballynahinch").[[34]](#cite_note-:11-35)

Britain seized on the rebellion to abolish the [Irish Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Ireland "Parliament of Ireland"), unlamented in Belfast, and to [incorporate Ireland](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1800 "Acts of Union 1800") in a [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland").[[35]](#cite_note-:25-36) In 1832, British [parliamentary reform](/wiki/Reform_Act_1832 "Reform Act 1832") permitted the town its first electoral contest[[36]](#cite_note-37) – an occasion for an early and lethal [sectarian](/wiki/Sectarianism "Sectarianism") riot.[[37]](#cite_note-:18-38): 87

### Industrial expansion, sectarian division

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Industrial expansion, sectarian division")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/High_Street%2C_Belfast_%285785358121%29.jpg/250px-High_Street%2C_Belfast_%285785358121%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:High_Street,_Belfast_(5785358121).jpg)

High Street, c. 1906

While other Irish towns experienced a loss of manufacturing, from the 1820s Belfast underwent rapid industrial expansion. After a cotton boom and bust, the town emerged as the global leader in the production of [linen](/wiki/Linen "Linen") goods (mill, and finishing, work largely employing women and children),[[38]](#cite_note-Belfast,_The_Making_of_the_City_32-39) winning the moniker "[Linenopolis](/wiki/Economy_of_Belfast "Economy of Belfast")".[[39]](#cite_note-40) Shipbuilding led the development of heavier industry.[[40]](#cite_note-Johnson2020-41) By the 1900s, her shipyards were building up to a quarter of the total United Kingdom tonnage,[[41]](#cite_note-42) and on the eve of the [Great War](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), in 1914, close one eighth of world production.[[25]](#cite_note-:26-26): 167 This included from the yard of [Harland & Wolff](/wiki/Harland_%26_Wolff "Harland & Wolff") the ill-fated RMS *[Titanic](/wiki/Titanic "Titanic"),* at the time of her launch in 1911 the largest ship afloat.*[[42]](#cite_note-Titanic_In_History-43)* Other major export industries included textile machinery, rope-making, tobacco and mineral waters.[[17]](#cite_note-:21-18): 59–88

Industry drew in a new Catholic population settling largely in the west of the town—refugees from a rural poverty intensified by Belfast's mechanisation of spinning and weaving and, in the 1840s, by [famine](/wiki/Great_Famine_(Ireland) "Great Famine (Ireland)").[[43]](#cite_note-44) The plentiful supply of cheap labour helped attract English and Scottish capital to Belfast, but it was also a cause of insecurity.[[44]](#cite_note-:4-45) Protestant workers organised and dominated the apprenticed trades[[45]](#cite_note-:9-46) and gave a new lease of life to the once largely rural [Orange Order](/wiki/Orange_Order "Orange Order").[[46]](#cite_note-:8-47)[[47]](#cite_note-48) Sectarian tensions, which frequently broke out in riots and workplace expulsions, were also driven by the "constitutional question": the prospect of a restored Irish parliament in which Protestants (and northern industry) feared being a minority interest.[[45]](#cite_note-:9-46)

On 28 September 1912, [unionists](/wiki/Unionism_in_Ireland "Unionism in Ireland") massed at [Belfast's City Hall](/wiki/Belfast_City_Hall "Belfast City Hall") to sign the [Ulster Covenant](/wiki/Ulster_Covenant "Ulster Covenant"), pledging to use "all means which may be found necessary to defeat the present [conspiracy](/wiki/Conspiracy_(political) "Conspiracy (political)") to set up a [Home Rule](/wiki/Home_Rule "Home Rule") Parliament in Ireland".[[48]](#cite_note-49) This was followed by the drilling and eventual arming of a 100,000-strong [Ulster Volunteer Force](/wiki/Ulster_Volunteers "Ulster Volunteers") (UVF).[[49]](#cite_note-50) The immediate crisis was averted by the onset of the [Great War](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"). The UVF formed the [36th (Ulster) Division](/wiki/36th_(Ulster)_Division "36th (Ulster) Division") whose sacrifices in the [Battle of the Somme](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Somme "Battle of the Somme") continue to be commemorated in the city by unionist and [loyalist](/wiki/Ulster_loyalism "Ulster loyalism") organisations.[[50]](#cite_note-51)

In 1920–22, as Belfast emerged as the capital of the six counties remaining as [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland") in the United Kingdom, there was [widespread violence](/wiki/The_Troubles_in_Ulster_(1920%E2%80%931922) "The Troubles in Ulster (1920–1922)"). 8,000 "disloyal" workers were driven from their jobs in the shipyards:[[51]](#cite_note-52) in addition to Catholics, "rotten Prods" – Protestants whose labour politics disregarded sectarian distinctions.[[52]](#cite_note-:13-53): 104–108 Gun battles, grenade attacks and house burnings contributed to as many as 500 deaths.[[53]](#cite_note-54) A curfew remained in force until 1924.[[35]](#cite_note-:25-36): 194

The lines drawn saw off the challenge to "unionist unity" posed by [labour](/wiki/Belfast_Labour_Party "Belfast Labour Party").[[54]](#cite_note-55) Industry had been paralysed by [strikes in 1907](/wiki/1907_Belfast_Dock_strike "1907 Belfast Dock strike") and again in 1919 (when the city was effectively policed by strikers).[[55]](#cite_note-56) Until "troubles" returned at the end of the 1960s, it was not uncommon in Belfast for the [Ulster Unionist Party](/wiki/Ulster_Unionist_Party "Ulster Unionist Party") to have its [council](/wiki/Category:Belfast_City_Council_elections "Category:Belfast City Council elections") and [parliamentary](/wiki/Category:General_elections_to_the_Parliament_of_Northern_Ireland "Category:General elections to the Parliament of Northern Ireland") candidates returned unopposed.[[56]](#cite_note-57)[[57]](#cite_note-58)

In 1932, the opening of the new buildings for [Northern Ireland's devolved Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Northern_Ireland "Parliament of Northern Ireland") at [Stormont](/wiki/Stormont_Buildings "Stormont Buildings") was overshadowed by the protests of the unemployed and ten days of running street battles with the police. The government conceded increases in [Outdoor Relief](/wiki/Outdoor_relief "Outdoor relief"), but labour unity was short lived.[[37]](#cite_note-:18-38): 219–220 In 1935, celebrations of [King George V](/wiki/George_V "George V")'s Jubilee and of the annual Twelfth were followed by deadly riots and expulsions, a sectarian logic that extended itself to the interpretation of darkening events in Europe.[[37]](#cite_note-:18-38): 226–233 [Labour candidates](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Labour_Party "Northern Ireland Labour Party") found support for the [anti-clerical](/wiki/Anti-clericalism "Anti-clericalism") [Spanish Republic](/wiki/Second_Spanish_Republic "Second Spanish Republic") (marked today by a [*No Pasaran!*](/wiki/No_Pasaran "No Pasaran") stained glass window in City Hall)[[58]](#cite_note-59) characterised as another instance of [No-Popery](/wiki/Popery "Popery").[[59]](#cite_note-60)

In 1938, nearly a third of industrial workers were unemployed, [malnutrition](/wiki/Malnutrition "Malnutrition") was a major issue, and at 9.6% the city's [infant mortality](/wiki/Infant_mortality "Infant mortality") rate (compared with 5.9% in [Sheffield](/wiki/Sheffield "Sheffield"), England) was among the highest in United Kingdom.[[60]](#cite_note-episode_48-61)

### The Blitz and post-war development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: The Blitz and post-war development")]

Main article: [Belfast Blitz](/wiki/Belfast_Blitz "Belfast Blitz")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/AIR_RAID_DAMAGE_IN_THE_UNITED_KINGDOM_1939-1945_-_H_9476.jpg/250px-AIR_RAID_DAMAGE_IN_THE_UNITED_KINGDOM_1939-1945_-_H_9476.jpg)](/wiki/File:AIR_RAID_DAMAGE_IN_THE_UNITED_KINGDOM_1939-1945_-_H_9476.jpg)

Aftermath of the Blitz in May 1941

In the spring of 1941, the German *[Luftwaffe](/wiki/Luftwaffe "Luftwaffe")* appeared four times over Belfast. In addition to the shipyards and the [Short & Harland](/wiki/Short_and_Harland "Short and Harland") aircraft factory, the [Belfast Blitz](/wiki/Belfast_Blitz "Belfast Blitz") severely damaged or destroyed more than half the city's housing stock, and devastated the old town centre around High Street. In the greatest loss of life in any air raid outside of London, more than a thousand people were killed.[[61]](#cite_note-BBC11042001-62)[[62]](#cite_note-63)

At the end of the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"), the Unionist government undertook programmes of ["slum clearance](/wiki/Slum_clearance_in_the_United_Kingdom "Slum clearance in the United Kingdom")" (the Blitz had exposed the "uninhabitable" condition of much of the city's housing) which involved decanting populations out of mill and factory built red-brick terraces and into new peripheral housing estates.[[63]](#cite_note-64)[[64]](#cite_note-:2-65) At the same time, a British-funded [welfare state](/wiki/Welfare_state "Welfare state") "revolutionised access" to education and health care.[[65]](#cite_note-66) The resulting rise in expectations; together with the uncertainty caused by the decline of the city's [Victorian-era](/wiki/Victorian_era "Victorian era") industries, contributed to growing protest, and counter protest, in the 1960s over the [Unionist government](/wiki/Government_of_Northern_Ireland_(1921%E2%80%931972) "Government of Northern Ireland (1921–1972)")'s record on civil and political rights.[[66]](#cite_note-67)

### The Troubles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: The Troubles")]

Main article: [The Troubles](/wiki/The_Troubles "The Troubles")

For reasons that [nationalists](/wiki/Irish_nationalism "Irish nationalism") and [unionists](/wiki/Unionism_in_Ireland "Unionism in Ireland") dispute,[[67]](#cite_note-68) the public protests of the late 1960s soon gave way to communal violence (in which as many as 60,000 people were intimidated from their homes)[[68]](#cite_note-:0-69): 70 and to [loyalist](/wiki/Ulster_loyalism "Ulster loyalism") and [republican](/wiki/Irish_republicanism "Irish republicanism") [paramilitarism](/wiki/Paramilitary "Paramilitary"). Introduced onto the streets in August 1969, the [British Army](/wiki/Operation_Banner "Operation Banner") committed to the longest continuous deployment in its history, [Operation Banner](/wiki/Operation_Banner "Operation Banner"). Beginning in 1970 with the [Falls curfew](/wiki/Falls_Curfew "Falls Curfew"), and followed in 1971 by [internment](/wiki/Operation_Demetrius "Operation Demetrius"), this included [counterinsurgency](/wiki/Counterinsurgency "Counterinsurgency") measures directed chiefly at the [Provisional Irish Republican Army](/wiki/Provisional_Irish_Republican_Army "Provisional Irish Republican Army"). The PIRA characterised their operations, including the bombing of Belfast's commercial centre, as a struggle against British occupation.[[69]](#cite_note-70)[[70]](#cite_note-71)

Preceded by loyalist and republican ceasefires, the 1998 ["Good Friday" Belfast Agreement](/wiki/Good_Friday_Agreement "Good Friday Agreement") returned a new [power-sharing](/wiki/Power_sharing "Power sharing") [legislative assembly](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Assembly "Northern Ireland Assembly") and [executive](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Executive "Northern Ireland Executive") to Stormont.[[71]](#cite_note-72) In the intervening years in Belfast, some 20,000 people had been injured, and 1,500 killed.[[68]](#cite_note-:0-69): 73[[72]](#cite_note-73)

Eighty-five percent of the conflict-related deaths had occurred within 1,000 metres of the communal [interfaces](/wiki/Interface_area "Interface area"), largely in the north and west of the city.[[68]](#cite_note-:0-69): 73 The security barriers erected at these interfaces are an enduring physical legacy of the Troubles.[[73]](#cite_note-:1-74) The 14 neighbourhoods they separate are among the 20 most deprived [wards](/wiki/Ward_(electoral_subdivision) "Ward (electoral subdivision)") in Northern Ireland.[[74]](#cite_note-75) In May 2013, the [Northern Ireland Executive](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Executive "Northern Ireland Executive") committed to the removal of all peace lines by mutual consent.[[75]](#cite_note-76)[[76]](#cite_note-77) The target date of 2023 was passed with only a small number dismantled.[[77]](#cite_note-78)[[78]](#cite_note-79)

The more affluent districts escaped the worst of the violence, but the city centre was a major target. This was especially so during the first phase of the PIRA campaign in the early 1970s, when the organisation hoped to secure quick political results through maximum destruction.[[73]](#cite_note-:1-74): 331–332 Including [car bombs](/wiki/Car_bomb "Car bomb") and incendiaries, between 1969 and 1977 the city experienced 2,280 explosions.[[24]](#cite_note-:16-25): 58 In addition to the death and injury caused, they accelerated the loss of the city's Victorian fabric.[[79]](#cite_note-80)

### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 21st century")]

Since the turn of the century, the loss of employment and population in the city centre has been reversed.[[80]](#cite_note-81) This reflects the growth of the [service economy](/wiki/Service_economy "Service economy"), for which a new district has been developed on former dockland, the [Titanic Quarter](/wiki/Titanic_Quarter "Titanic Quarter"). The growing tourism sector paradoxically lists as attractions the [murals](/wiki/Murals_in_Northern_Ireland "Murals in Northern Ireland") and peace walls that echo the violence of the past.[[73]](#cite_note-:1-74): 350.352 In recent years, "Troubles tourism"[[31]](#cite_note-:3-32): 180–189 has presented visitors with new territorial markers: flags, murals and graffiti in which [loyalists](/wiki/Ulster_loyalism "Ulster loyalism") and [republicans](/wiki/Irish_republicanism "Irish republicanism") take opposing sides in the [Israeli-Palestinian conflict](/wiki/Israeli%E2%80%93Palestinian_conflict "Israeli–Palestinian conflict").[[81]](#cite_note-82)

The demographic balance of some areas has been changed by immigration (according to the 2021 census just under 10% of the city's population was born outside the British Isles),[[82]](#cite_note-:5-83) by local differences in births and deaths between Catholics and Protestants, and by a growing number of, particularly younger, people no longer willing to self-identify on traditional lines.[[64]](#cite_note-:2-65)

In 1997, unionists lost overall control of [Belfast City Council](/wiki/Belfast_City_Council "Belfast City Council") for the first time in its history. The election in 2011 saw Irish nationalist councillors outnumber unionist councillors, with [Sinn Féin](/wiki/Sinn_F%C3%A9in "Sinn Féin") becoming the largest party, and the cross-community [Alliance Party](/wiki/Alliance_Party_of_Northern_Ireland "Alliance Party of Northern Ireland") holding the balance of power.[[83]](#cite_note-84)

In the [2016 Brexit referendum](/wiki/2016_United_Kingdom_European_Union_membership_referendum "2016 United Kingdom European Union membership referendum"), Belfast's four parliamentary constituencies returned a substantial majority (60 percent) for remaining within the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"), as did Northern Ireland as a whole (55.8), the only [UK region](/wiki/UK_regions "UK regions") outside London and Scotland to do so.[[84]](#cite_note-85) In February 2022, the [Democratic Unionist Party](/wiki/Democratic_Unionist_Party "Democratic Unionist Party"), which had actively campaigned for Brexit, withdrew from the power-sharing executive and collapsed the Stormont institutions to protest the 2020 UK-EU [Northern Ireland Protocol](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Protocol "Northern Ireland Protocol"). With the promise of equal access to the British and European markets, this designates Belfast as a point of entry to the [European Single Market](/wiki/European_single_market "European single market") within whose regulatory framework local producers will continue to operate.[[85]](#cite_note-86) After two years, the standoff was resolved with an agreement to eliminate routine checks on UK-destined goods.[[86]](#cite_note-87)

## Cityscape

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Cityscape")]

### Location and topography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Location and topography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Belfast_with_Lough_by_Sentinel-2.jpg/250px-Belfast_with_Lough_by_Sentinel-2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Belfast_with_Lough_by_Sentinel-2.jpg)

Satellite image of Belfast with Lough

Belfast is at the mouth of the River Lagan at the head of Belfast Lough open through the [North Channel](/wiki/North_Channel_(Great_Britain_and_Ireland) "North Channel (Great Britain and Ireland)") to the [Irish Sea](/wiki/Irish_Sea "Irish Sea") and to the North Atlantic. In the course of the 19th century, the location's estuarine features were re-engineered. With dredging and reclamation, the lough was made to accommodate a deep sea port, and extensive shipyards.[[87]](#cite_note-:20-88) The Lagan was banked (in 1994 a [weir](/wiki/Lagan_Weir "Lagan Weir") raised its water level to cover what remained of the tidal mud flats)[[88]](#cite_note-89) and its various tributaries were culverted[[89]](#cite_note-90) On the model pioneered in 2008 by the Connswater Community Greenway some, including the course of the Farset, are now being considered for "daylighting".[[90]](#cite_note-91)

It remains the case that much of the city centre is built on an estuarine bed of "sleech": silt, peat, mud and—a source the city's ubiquitous red brick— soft clay, that presents a challenge for high-rise construction.[[91]](#cite_note-92) (In 2007 this unstable foundation persuaded [St Anne's Cathedral](/wiki/St_Anne%27s_Cathedral,_Belfast "St Anne's Cathedral, Belfast") to abandon plans for a bell tower and substitute a lightweight steel spire).[[92]](#cite_note-93) The city centre is also subject to tidal flood risk. Rising sea levels could mean, that without significant investment, flooding in the coming decades will be persistent.[[93]](#cite_note-94)

The city is overlooked on the [County Antrim](/wiki/County_Antrim "County Antrim") side (to the north and northwest) by a precipitous [basalt](/wiki/Basalt "Basalt") [escarpment](/wiki/Escarpment "Escarpment")—the near continuous line of [Divis](/wiki/Divis "Divis") Mountain (478 m), [Black Mountain](/wiki/Black_Mountain_(Belfast) "Black Mountain (Belfast)") (389 m) and [Cavehill](/wiki/Cavehill "Cavehill") (368 m)—whose "heathery slopes and hanging fields are visible from almost any part of the city".[[87]](#cite_note-:20-88): 13 From [County Down](/wiki/County_Down "County Down") side (on the south and south east) it is flanked by the lower-lying [Castlereagh](/wiki/Castlereagh_(borough) "Castlereagh (borough)") and [Hollywood](/wiki/Holywood,_County_Down "Holywood, County Down") hills. The sand and gravel [Malone](/wiki/Malone_Road "Malone Road") Ridge extends up river to the south-west.

### North Belfast and Shankill

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: North Belfast and Shankill")]

From 1820, Belfast began to spread rapidly beyond its 18th century limits. To the north, it stretched out along roads which drew into the town migrants from Scots-settled hinterland of [County Antrim](/wiki/County_Antrim "County Antrim").[[44]](#cite_note-:4-45) Largely Presbyterian, they enveloped a number of Catholic-occupied "[mill-row](/wiki/Tied_cottage "Tied cottage")" clusters: [New Lodge](/wiki/New_Lodge,_Belfast "New Lodge, Belfast"), [Ardoyne](/wiki/Ardoyne "Ardoyne") and "the Marrowbone".[[94]](#cite_note-95)[[95]](#cite_note-96) Together with areas of more substantial housing in the [Oldpark district](/wiki/Oldpark_(District_Electoral_Area) "Oldpark (District Electoral Area)"), these are wedged between Protestant working-class housing stretching from [Tiger's Bay](/wiki/Shore_Road,_Belfast "Shore Road, Belfast") out the Shore Road on one side, and up the [Shankill](/wiki/Shankill_Road "Shankill Road") (the original Antrim Road) on the other.[[96]](#cite_note-97)

The Greater Shankill area, including [Crumlin](/wiki/Crumlin_Road "Crumlin Road") and [Woodvale](/wiki/Court_(District_Electoral_Area) "Court (District Electoral Area)"), is over the line from the [Belfast North](/wiki/Belfast_North_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Belfast North (UK Parliament constituency)") parliamentary/assembly constituency, but is physically separated from the rest of [Belfast West](/wiki/Belfast_West_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Belfast West (UK Parliament constituency)") by an extensive series of separation barriers[[97]](#cite_note-98)—[peace walls](/wiki/Peace_lines "Peace lines")—owned (together with five daytime gates into the [Falls area](/wiki/Falls_Road,_Belfast "Falls Road, Belfast")) by the [Department of Justice](/wiki/Department_of_Justice_(Northern_Ireland) "Department of Justice (Northern Ireland)").[[98]](#cite_note-99) These include Cupar Way where tourists are informed that, at 45 feet, the barrier is "three times higher than the [Berlin Wall](/wiki/Berlin_Wall "Berlin Wall") and has been in place for twice as long".[[99]](#cite_note-100)

With other working-class districts, Shankill suffered from the "collapse of old industrial Belfast".[[100]](#cite_note-:03-101) But it was also greatly affected from the 1960s by the city's most ambitious programme of "slum clearance". Red-brick, "two up, two down" terraced streets, typical of 19th century working-class housing, were replaced with flats, maisonettes, and car parks but few facilities. In a period of twenty years, due largely to redevelopment, 50,000 residents left the area leaving an aging population of 26,000[[101]](#cite_note-102)[[100]](#cite_note-:03-101) and more than 100 acres of wasteland.[[102]](#cite_note-103)

Meanwhile, [road schemes](/wiki/Belfast_Urban_Motorway "Belfast Urban Motorway"), including the terminus of the [M1 motorway](/wiki/M1_motorway_(Northern_Ireland) "M1 motorway (Northern Ireland)") and the [Westlink](/wiki/Westlink_(road) "Westlink (road)"), demolished a mixed dockland community, [Sailortown](/wiki/Sailortown_(Belfast) "Sailortown (Belfast)"), and severed the streets linking the Shankill area and the rest of both north and west Belfast to the city centre.[[103]](#cite_note-104)[[104]](#cite_note-105)

New "green field" housing estates were built on the outer edges of the city. The onset of the Troubles overwhelmed attempts to promote these as "mixed" neighbourhoods so that the largest of these developments on the city's northern edge, [Rathcoole](/wiki/Rathcoole_(Newtownabbey) "Rathcoole (Newtownabbey)"), rapidly solidified as a [loyalist](/wiki/Ulster_loyalism "Ulster loyalism") community.[[105]](#cite_note-106) In 2004, it was estimated that 98% of public housing in Belfast was divided along religious lines.[[106]](#cite_note-SelfImposed-107)

Among the principal landmarks of north Belfast are the [Crumlin Road Gaol](/wiki/HM_Prison_Belfast "HM Prison Belfast") (1845) now a major visitor attraction, [Belfast Royal Academy](/wiki/Belfast_Royal_Academy "Belfast Royal Academy") (1785) - the oldest school in the city, [St Malachy's College](/wiki/St_Malachy%27s_College "St Malachy's College") (1833), [Holy Cross Church, Ardoyne](/wiki/Holy_Cross_Church,_Ardoyne "Holy Cross Church, Ardoyne") (1902), [Waterworks Park](https://www.belfastcity.gov.uk/things-to-do/parks-and-open-spaces/a-z-parks/waterworks) (1889), and [Belfast Zoo](/wiki/Belfast_Zoo "Belfast Zoo") (1934).

### West Belfast

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: West Belfast")]

In the mid-19th century rural poverty and famine drove large numbers of Catholic tenant farmers, landless labourers and their families toward Belfast. Their route brought them down the [Falls Road](/wiki/Falls_Road,_Belfast "Falls Road, Belfast") and into what are now remnants of an older Catholic enclave around [St Mary's Church](/wiki/St_Mary%27s_Church,_Belfast "St Mary's Church, Belfast"), the town's first Catholic chapel (opened in 1784 with Presbyterian subscriptions),[[107]](#cite_note-108) and [Smithfield Market](/wiki/Smithfield_Market_and_Library_Quarter,_Belfast "Smithfield Market and Library Quarter, Belfast").[[44]](#cite_note-:4-45) Eventually, an entire west side of the city, stretching up the Falls Road, along the [Springfield Road](/wiki/Springfield_Road "Springfield Road") (encompassing the new housing estates built 1950s and 60s: Highfield, New Barnsley, Ballymurphy, Whiterock and Turf Lodge) and out past [Andersonstown](/wiki/Andersonstown "Andersonstown") on the Stewartstown Road toward [Poleglass](/wiki/Poleglass "Poleglass"), became near-exclusively Catholic and, in political terms, nationalist.

Reflecting the nature of available employment as mill workers, domestics and shop assistants, the population, initially, was disproportionately female. Further opportunities for women on the Falls Road arose through developments in education and public health. In 1900, the [Dominican Order](/wiki/Dominican_Order "Dominican Order") opened [St Mary's [Teacher] Training College](/wiki/St_Mary%27s_University_College,_Belfast "St Mary's University College, Belfast"), and in 1903 [King Edward VII](/wiki/Edward_VII "Edward VII") opened the [Royal Victoria Hospital](/wiki/Royal_Victoria_Hospital,_Belfast "Royal Victoria Hospital, Belfast") at the junction with the Grosvenor Road.[[108]](#cite_note-clarke-109) Extensively redeveloped and expanded, the hospital has a staff of more than 8,500.[[109]](#cite_note-110)

Landmarks in the area include the [Gothic-revival](/wiki/Gothic_Revival_architecture "Gothic Revival architecture") [St Peter's Cathedral](/wiki/St_Peter%27s_Cathedral,_Belfast "St Peter's Cathedral, Belfast") (1866, signature twin spires added in 1886);[[110]](#cite_note-111) [Clonard Monastery](/wiki/Clonard_Monastery "Clonard Monastery") (1911), the Conway Mill (1853/1901, re-developed as a community enterprise, arts and education centre in 1983);[[111]](#cite_note-112) [Belfast City Cemetery](/wiki/Belfast_City_Cemetery "Belfast City Cemetery") (1869) and, best known for its republican graves, [Milltown Cemetery](/wiki/Milltown_Cemetery "Milltown Cemetery") (1869).

The area's greatest visitor attractions are its wall and gable-end murals. In contrast to those in loyalist areas, where Israel is typically the only outside reference, these range more freely beyond the local conflict frequently expressing solidarity with [Palestinians](/wiki/Palestinians "Palestinians"), with [Cuba](/wiki/Cuba "Cuba"), and with [Basque](/wiki/Basque_Country_(autonomous_community) "Basque Country (autonomous community)") and [Catalan](/wiki/Catalonia "Catalonia") separatists.[[112]](#cite_note-113)[[113]](#cite_note-114)

### South Belfast

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: South Belfast")]

West Belfast is separated from South Belfast, and from the otherwise abutting loyalist districts of [Sandy Row](/wiki/Sandy_Row "Sandy Row") and the [Donegall Road](/wiki/Donegall_Road "Donegall Road"), by rail lines, the [M1 Motorway](/wiki/M1_motorway_(Northern_Ireland) "M1 motorway (Northern Ireland)") (to Dublin and the west); industrial and retail parks, and the remnants of the [Blackstaff](/wiki/Blackstaff_River "Blackstaff River") (Owenvarra) bog meadows.

Belfast began stretching up-river in the 1840s and 50s: out the [Ormeau](/wiki/Ormeau_Road "Ormeau Road") and [Lisburn roads](/wiki/Lisburn_Road "Lisburn Road") and, between them, running along a ridge of higher ground, the [Malone Road](/wiki/Malone_Road "Malone Road"). From "leafy" avenues of increasingly substantial (and in the course of time "mixed") housing, the Upper Malone broadened out into areas of parkland and villas.

Further out still, where they did not survive as public parks, from the 1960s the great-house demesnes of the city's former mill-owners and industrialists were developed for public housing: loyalist estates such as Seymour Hill and Belvoir. Meanwhile, in Malone and along the river embankments, new houses and apartment blocks have been squeezed in, increasing the general housing density.[[114]](#cite_note-115)

Beyond the Queen's University area the area's principal landmarks are the 15-storey tower block of [Belfast City Hospital](/wiki/Belfast_City_Hospital "Belfast City Hospital") (1986) on the Lisburn Road, and the Lagan Valley Regional Park through which a [towpath](/wiki/Towpath "Towpath") extends from the City-centre quayside to Lisburn.[[115]](#cite_note-:12-116)

Northern Ireland's [three permanent diplomatic missions](/wiki/List_of_diplomatic_missions_in_Northern_Ireland "List of diplomatic missions in Northern Ireland") are situated on the Malone Road, the consulates of China,[[116]](#cite_note-117) Poland[[117]](#cite_note-118) and the United States.[[118]](#cite_note-119)

### East Belfast

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: East Belfast")]

The first district on the right bank of the Lagan (the [County Down](/wiki/County_Down "County Down") side) to be incorporated in Belfast was [Ballymacarrett](/wiki/Ballymacarrett "Ballymacarrett") in 1853.[[119]](#cite_note-120) [Harland & Wolff](/wiki/Harland_%26_Wolff "Harland & Wolff"), whose gantry cranes, [Samson & Goliath](/wiki/Samson_and_Goliath_(cranes) "Samson and Goliath (cranes)"), tower over the area, was long the mainstay of employment — although less securely so for the [townland](/wiki/Townland "Townland")'s Catholics (In 1970, when the yard still had a workforce of 10,000, only 400 Catholics were employed).[[37]](#cite_note-:18-38): 280 Tolerated in periods of expansion as [navvies](/wiki/Navvy "Navvy") and casual labourers,[[52]](#cite_note-:13-53): 87–88 they concentrated in a small enclave, the [Short Strand](/wiki/Short_Strand "Short Strand"), which has continued into this century to feature as a sectarian flashpoint.[[120]](#cite_note-121)[[121]](#cite_note-122) Home to around 2,500 people, it is the only distinctly nationalist area in the east of the river.[[122]](#cite_note-123)

East Belfast developed from the [Queens Bridge](/wiki/Queen%27s_Bridge,_Belfast "Queen's Bridge, Belfast") (1843), through Ballymacarrett, east along the [Newtownards Road](/wiki/Newtownards_Road "Newtownards Road") and north (along the east shore of the Lough) up the [Holywood](/wiki/Holywood,_County_Down "Holywood, County Down") Road; and from the [Albert Bridge](/wiki/Albert_Bridge,_Belfast "Albert Bridge, Belfast") (1890) south east out the [Cregagh](/wiki/Cregagh "Cregagh") and Castlereagh roads. The further out, the more substantial, and less religiously segregated, the housing until again encountering the city's outer ring of public housing estates: loyalist Knocknagoney, [Lisnasharragh](/wiki/Lisnasharragh_(District_Electoral_Area) "Lisnasharragh (District Electoral Area)"), and [Tullycarnet](/wiki/Tullycarnet "Tullycarnet").

This century, efforts have been made to add to East Belfast's two obvious visitor attractions: Samson & Goliath (the "banana yellow" Harland & Wolff cranes date only from the early 1970s)[[52]](#cite_note-:13-53): 79 and the [Parliament Buildings at Stormont](/wiki/Parliament_Buildings_(Northern_Ireland) "Parliament Buildings (Northern Ireland)"). What is marketed now as EastSide, features, at the intersection of the Connswater and Comber Greenways and next to the EastSide Visitor Centre, [CS Lewis](/wiki/C._S._Lewis "C. S. Lewis") Square (2017), named and themed in honour of the local author of *[The Chronicles of Narnia](/wiki/The_Chronicles_of_Narnia "The Chronicles of Narnia").*[[123]](#cite_note-124) Next to the former the Harland & Wolff Drawing Offices (now an hotel), stands the "cultural nucleus to Titanic Quarter", [*Titanic* Belfast](/wiki/Titanic_Belfast "Titanic Belfast") (2012) whose interactive galleries tell the liner's ill-fated story.[[124]](#cite_note-125)

In 2015, the [Orange Order](/wiki/Orange_Order "Orange Order") opened the Museum of Orange Heritage on the Cregagh Road with the aim of educating the wider public about "the origins, traditions and continued relevance" of the parading institution.[[125]](#cite_note-:24-126)

### City Centre

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: City Centre")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/St._Georges_Market_in_Belfast_2017.jpg/250px-St._Georges_Market_in_Belfast_2017.jpg)](/wiki/File:St._Georges_Market_in_Belfast_2017.jpg)

Inside St. Georges Market in 2017

[Belfast City Centre](/wiki/Belfast_City_Centre "Belfast City Centre") is roughly bounded by the ring roads constructed since the 1970s: the [M3](/wiki/M3_motorway_(Northern_Ireland) "M3 motorway (Northern Ireland)") which sweeps across the dockland to the north; the Westlink that connects to the [M1](/wiki/M1_motorway_(Northern_Ireland) "M1 motorway (Northern Ireland)") for points south and west; and, with less certainty, the Bruce Street and Bankmore connectors that tie back toward the Lagan at the Gasworks Business Park and the beginning of the Ormeau Road. This embraces "the Markets", the one remaining inner-city area of housing. Of the various markets, including those for the sale and shipping of livestock, from which it derives its name, only one survives, the former produce market, [St George's Market](/wiki/St_George%27s_Market "St George's Market"),[[126]](#cite_note-127) now a food and craft market popular with visitors to the city.[[127]](#cite_note-128)

#### Architectural heritage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Architectural heritage")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Clifton_House%2C_Belfast%2C_July_2010_%2803%29.JPG/250px-Clifton_House%2C_Belfast%2C_July_2010_%2803%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Clifton_House,_Belfast,_July_2010_(03).JPG)

Belfast Charitable Society, [Clifton House](/wiki/Clifton_House,_Belfast "Clifton House, Belfast"), 1774

Among surviving elements of the pre-Victorian town are the [Belfast Entries](/wiki/The_Belfast_Entries "The Belfast Entries"), 17th-century alleyways off High Street, including, in Winecellar Entry, White's Tavern (rebuilt 1790); the elliptical First [Presbyterian (Non-Subscribing)](/wiki/Non-subscribing_Presbyterian_Church_of_Ireland "Non-subscribing Presbyterian Church of Ireland") Church (1781–83) in Rosemary Street (whose members led the abolitionist charge against Greg and Cunningham);[[128]](#cite_note-129) the [Assembly Rooms](/wiki/Assembly_Rooms,_Belfast "Assembly Rooms, Belfast") (1769, 1776, 1845) on Bridge Street; [St George's Church of Ireland](/wiki/St_George%27s_Church,_Belfast "St George's Church, Belfast") (1816) on the High Street site of the old Corporation Church; [St Mary's Church](/wiki/St_Mary%27s_Church,_Belfast "St Mary's Church, Belfast") (1782) in Chapel Lane, which is the oldest Catholic church in the city. The oldest public building in Belfast, [Clifton House](/wiki/Clifton_House,_Belfast "Clifton House, Belfast") (1771–74), the [Belfast Charitable Society](/wiki/Belfast_Charitable_Society "Belfast Charitable Society") [poorhouse](/wiki/Poorhouse "Poorhouse"), is on North Queen Street. It is now partly cut off from the city centre by arterial roads. In addition there are small sets of city-centre [Georgian](/wiki/Georgian_architecture "Georgian architecture") terraces.[[129]](#cite_note-:42-130)

Of the much larger Victorian city a substantial legacy has survived the [Blitz](/wiki/Belfast_Blitz "Belfast Blitz"), The Troubles and planning and development. Among the more notable examples are [St Malachy's Roman Catholic Church](/wiki/St_Malachy%27s_Church,_Belfast "St Malachy's Church, Belfast") (1844) and the original college building of [Queen's University Belfast](/wiki/Queen%27s_University_Belfast "Queen's University Belfast") (1849), both in a [Tudor style](/wiki/Tudor_style_architecture "Tudor style architecture"); the Palm House in the [Botanic Gardens](/wiki/Botanic_Gardens_(Belfast) "Botanic Gardens (Belfast)") (1852); the [Renaissance revival](/wiki/Renaissance_Revival_architecture "Renaissance Revival architecture") [Union Theological College](/wiki/Union_Theological_College "Union Theological College") (1853) and Ulster Bank (now [Merchant Hotel](/wiki/Merchant_Hotel_(Belfast) "Merchant Hotel (Belfast)")) (1860); the [Italianate](/wiki/Italianate_architecture "Italianate architecture") [Ulster Hall](/wiki/Ulster_Hall "Ulster Hall") (1862), and the National Trust restored ornate [Crown Liquor Saloon](/wiki/Crown_Liquor_Saloon "Crown Liquor Saloon") (1885, 1898) (a setting for the classic film, *[Odd Man Out](/wiki/Odd_Man_Out "Odd Man Out")*, starring [James Mason](/wiki/James_Mason "James Mason"));[[130]](#cite_note-131) the oriental-themed [Grand Opera House](/wiki/Grand_Opera_House_(Belfast) "Grand Opera House (Belfast)") (1895) (bombed several times during the Troubles), and the Romanesque revival [St. Patrick's Catholic Church](/wiki/St_Patrick%27s_Church,_Belfast "St Patrick's Church, Belfast") in Donegall Street (1877).[[129]](#cite_note-:42-130)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/Scottish_Provident_Institution%2C_Belfast.jpg/250px-Scottish_Provident_Institution%2C_Belfast.jpg)](/wiki/File:Scottish_Provident_Institution,_Belfast.jpg)

Scottish Provident Institution, 1902

The [Baroque revival](/wiki/Baroque_Revival_architecture "Baroque Revival architecture") [City Hall](/wiki/Belfast_City_Hall "Belfast City Hall") was finished in 1906 on the site of the former White Linen Hall, and was built to reflect Belfast's city status, granted by [Queen Victoria](/wiki/Queen_Victoria "Queen Victoria") in 1888. Its [Edwardian](/wiki/Edwardian_Baroque_architecture "Edwardian Baroque architecture") design influenced the [Victoria Memorial](/wiki/Victoria_Memorial_(India) "Victoria Memorial (India)") in [Calcutta](/wiki/Kolkata "Kolkata"), India, and [Durban](/wiki/Durban "Durban") City Hall in South Africa.[[131]](#cite_note-132) The dome is 173 ft (53 m) high and figures above the door state "[Hibernia](/wiki/Hibernia "Hibernia") encouraging and promoting the Commerce and Arts of the City".[[132]](#cite_note-Go_To_Historic_Buildings2-133)

Across from the City Hall, on Donegall Sq. West, is the Renaissance and Baroque revival Scottish Provident Institution (1902). On the east side, a branch of the [Ulster Bank](/wiki/Ulster_Bank "Ulster Bank") is built behind the classical portico of a former Methodist church dating from 1846. On the north side, facing the City Hall is the once palatial, copper-domed, "Royal Irish Linen Warehouse" of Robinson & Cleaver (1888), until its closure in 1984, the city's premier department store.[[133]](#cite_note-134) Next door, its successor, [Marks and Spencers](/wiki/Marks_%26_Spencer "Marks & Spencer"), is housed behind the red sandstone, Florentine Gothic, facade (1869) of a rival linen business that was burned out in the [Blitz](/wiki/Belfast_Blitz "Belfast Blitz").[[134]](#cite_note-135)

Built in the [Romanesque-style](/wiki/Romanesque_architecture "Romanesque architecture") on the site of an earlier [neo-classical](/wiki/Neoclassical_architecture "Neoclassical architecture") church, [St Anne's Church of Ireland Cathedral](/wiki/St_Anne%27s_Cathedral,_Belfast "St Anne's Cathedral, Belfast") was consecrated in 1904. The north transept, featuring on its exterior "the largest [Celtic cross](/wiki/Celtic_cross "Celtic cross") in Ireland",[[135]](#cite_note-136) was completed in 1981, and a final addition, a 40-metre stainless steel "Spire of Hope" was installed in 2007.[[136]](#cite_note-137)

The neoclassical [Royal Courts of Justice](/wiki/Royal_Courts_of_Justice,_Belfast "Royal Courts of Justice, Belfast") were opened on Oxford Street in 1933.[[137]](#cite_note-Royal_Courts_of_Justice-138)

#### Redevelopment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Redevelopment")]

The opening [Victoria Square Shopping Centre](/wiki/Victoria_Square_Shopping_Centre "Victoria Square Shopping Centre") in 2008 was to symbolise the rebound of the city centre since its days as a restricted security zone during the Troubles.[[138]](#cite_note-139) But retail footfall in the centre is limited by competition with out-of-town shopping centres and with internet retailing. As of November 2023, footfall had not recovered pre-[COVID pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic") levels.[[139]](#cite_note-140) There are compensating trends: the growth in tourism and hospitality which has included a sustained boom in hotel construction.[[140]](#cite_note-141)

The City Council also talks of a "residential-led regeneration".[[141]](#cite_note-142)[[142]](#cite_note-143) New townhouse and apartments schemes are being developed for the city's quays,[[143]](#cite_note-144) and for Titanic Quarter.[[144]](#cite_note-145) The completion in 2023 of Ulster University's enhanced Belfast campus (in "one of the largest higher education capital builds in Europe")[[145]](#cite_note-Ulster280723-146) and the determination of Queen's University to compete with the private sector in the provision of student housing,[[146]](#cite_note-147) has fostered the construction downtown of multiple new student residences.[[147]](#cite_note-148)

#### Rough sleeping and homelessness

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Rough sleeping and homelessness")]

People can be found sleeping rough on the streets of the city centre. Numbers, while growing, may be comparatively small for a city of its size in the British Isles. In 2022, counts and estimates by the [Northern Ireland Housing Executive](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Housing_Executive "Northern Ireland Housing Executive") identified a total of 26 rough sleepers in Belfast.[[148]](#cite_note-149) This is against a background (in 2023) of 2,317 people (0.67% of residents) presenting as homeless, many of whom are in temporary accommodation and shelters.[[149]](#cite_note-150) Such figures, however, do not include all those living in severely overcrowded conditions, involuntarily sharing with other households on a long-term basis, or sleeping rough in hidden locations.[[150]](#cite_note-151)[[151]](#cite_note-152)

### The "Quarters"

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: The \"Quarters\"")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Cathedral_St._Anne_Belfast.jpg/250px-Cathedral_St._Anne_Belfast.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cathedral_St._Anne_Belfast.jpg)

St Anne's Cathedral

Since 2001, buoyed by increasing numbers of tourists, the city council has promoted a number of cultural [quarters](/wiki/Belfast_quarters "Belfast quarters").

The [Cathedral Quarter](/wiki/Cathedral_Quarter,_Belfast "Cathedral Quarter, Belfast") comprises much of Belfast's old trade and warehousing district in the narrow streets and entries around [St Anne's Cathedral](/wiki/St_Anne%27s_Cathedral,_Belfast "St Anne's Cathedral, Belfast"), with a concentration of bars, beer gardens, clubs and restaurants (including two establishments claiming descent from the early town, White's and The Duke of York)[[152]](#cite_note-153) and performance spaces (most notably the Black Box and [Oh Yeah](/wiki/Oh_Yeah_(music_centre) "Oh Yeah (music centre)")).[[153]](#cite_note-154)[[154]](#cite_note-Cathedral_Quarter2-155) It hosts a yearly [visual and performing arts festival](/wiki/Cathedral_Quarter_Arts_Festival "Cathedral Quarter Arts Festival"). The adjoining [Custom House](/wiki/Custom_House,_Belfast "Custom House, Belfast") Square is one of the city's main outdoor venues for free concerts and street entertainment.

Without defined geographical boundaries, the [Gaeltacht Quarter](/wiki/Gaeltacht_Quarter,_Belfast "Gaeltacht Quarter, Belfast") encompasses [Irish-speaking](/wiki/Irish_language "Irish language") Belfast. (According to the 2021 census, 15.5% of people in the city have [some knowledge of Irish](/wiki/Irish_language_in_Northern_Ireland "Irish language in Northern Ireland"), 4% speak it daily).[[155]](#cite_note-:02-156) It is generally understood as an area around the Falls Road in west Belfast served by the [Cultúrlann McAdam Ó Fiaich](/wiki/Cult%C3%BArlann_McAdam_%C3%93_Fiaich "Cultúrlann McAdam Ó Fiaich") cultural centre.[[156]](#cite_note-157) It can be said to include, at the Skainos Centre in unionist east Belfast, [Turas](/wiki/Turas "Turas"), a project that promotes Irish through night classes and cultural events in the belief that "the language belongs to all".[[157]](#cite_note-158)

[![Aerial photo of urban sprawl, edged by green hills and sea shore, and bisected by a winding river.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/Belfast_Aerial.jpg/250px-Belfast_Aerial.jpg)](/wiki/File:Belfast_Aerial.jpg)

Aerial view of Belfast (2004)

The [Linen Quarter](/wiki/Linen_Quarter,_Belfast "Linen Quarter, Belfast")', an area south of City Hall once dominated by linen warehouses, now includes, in addition to cafés, bars and restaurants, a dozen hotels (including the 23-storey [Grand Central Hotel](/wiki/Grand_Central_Hotel_Belfast "Grand Central Hotel Belfast")), and the city's two principal Victorian-era cultural venues, the [Grand Opera House](/wiki/Grand_Opera_House,_Belfast "Grand Opera House, Belfast") and the [Ulster Hall](/wiki/Ulster_Hall "Ulster Hall").[[158]](#cite_note-159)

Moving further south along the so-called ["Golden Mile"](/wiki/Golden_Mile_(Belfast) "Golden Mile (Belfast)") of bars and clubs through [Shaftesbury Square](/wiki/Shaftesbury_Square "Shaftesbury Square"), there is the [Queen's [University] Quarter](/wiki/Queen%27s_Quarter,_Belfast "Queen's Quarter, Belfast"). In addition to the university (spread over 250 buildings, of which 120 are listed as being of architectural merit),[[159]](#cite_note-160) it is home to [Botanic Gardens](/wiki/Belfast_Botanic_Gardens "Belfast Botanic Gardens") and the [Ulster Museum](/wiki/Ulster_Museum "Ulster Museum").[[160]](#cite_note-161)

Finally, the [Titanic Quarter](/wiki/Titanic_Quarter,_Belfast "Titanic Quarter, Belfast") covers 0.75 km2 (185 acres) of reclaimed land adjacent to [Belfast Harbour](/wiki/Port_of_Belfast "Port of Belfast"), formerly known as *Queen's Island*. Named after [RMS *Titanic*](/wiki/RMS_Titanic "RMS Titanic"), launched here in 1911,[[42]](#cite_note-Titanic_In_History-43) work began in 2003 to transform some former shipyard land into "one of the largest waterfront developments in Europe".[[161]](#cite_note-Titanic_Quarter2-162) The current area houses [*Titanic* Belfast](/wiki/Titanic_Belfast "Titanic Belfast"), the [Public Records Office of Northern Ireland](/wiki/Public_Record_Office_of_Northern_Ireland "Public Record Office of Northern Ireland") (PRONI), two hotels, and multiple condo towers and shops, and the Titanic [film] Studios.[[162]](#cite_note-:15-163)

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Culture")]

Main article: [Culture of Belfast](/wiki/Culture_of_Belfast "Culture of Belfast")

### Arts venues and festivals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Arts venues and festivals")]

From [Georgian](/wiki/Georgian_era "Georgian era") Belfast, the city retains a civic legacy. In addition to [Clifton House](/wiki/Clifton_House,_Belfast "Clifton House, Belfast")[[163]](#cite_note-164) (Belfast Charitable Society, 1774), this includes the [Linen Hall Library](/wiki/Linen_Hall_Library "Linen Hall Library")[[164]](#cite_note-165) (Belfast Society for Promoting Knowledge, 1788), the [Ulster Museum](/wiki/Ulster_Museum "Ulster Museum") (founded in 1833 by the [Belfast Natural History Society](/wiki/Belfast_Natural_History_Society "Belfast Natural History Society") as the Belfast Municipal Museum and Art Gallery), and the [Botanic Gardens](/wiki/Botanic_Gardens_(Belfast) "Botanic Gardens (Belfast)")[[165]](#cite_note-Botanic_Gardens-166) (established in 1828 by the Belfast Botanic and Horticultural Society).[[165]](#cite_note-Botanic_Gardens-166) These remain important cultural venues: in the case of the Gardens, for outdoor festivities including the Belfast [Melā](/wiki/Mel%C4%81 "Melā"), the city's annual August celebration of global cultures.[[166]](#cite_note-167)

Of the many stage venues built in the nineteenth century, and film theatres built in the twentieth, there remains the [Ulster Hall](/wiki/Ulster_Hall "Ulster Hall") (1862),[[167]](#cite_note-168) which hosts concerts (including those of the [Ulster Orchestra](/wiki/Ulster_Orchestra "Ulster Orchestra")), [classical](/wiki/European_classical_music "European classical music") [recitals](/wiki/Concert#Recital "Concert") and party-political meetings; the [Grand Opera House](/wiki/Grand_Opera_House,_Belfast "Grand Opera House, Belfast")[[168]](#cite_note-169) (1895) badly damaged in bomb blasts in the early 1990s, restored and enlarged 2020; the [Strand Cinema](/wiki/Strand_Cinema "Strand Cinema")[[169]](#cite_note-170) (1935) now being developed as an arts centre;[[170]](#cite_note-Leebody-171) and the [Queens Film Theatre](/wiki/Queen%27s_Film_Theatre "Queen's Film Theatre") (QFT) (1968) focussed on [art house](/wiki/Art_film "Art film") and [world cinema](/wiki/World_cinema "World cinema").[[171]](#cite_note-172) The two independent cinemas offer their screens for the [Belfast Film Festival](/wiki/Belfast_Film_Festival "Belfast Film Festival") and the [Belfast International Arts Festival](/wiki/Belfast_Festival_at_Queen%27s "Belfast Festival at Queen's").

The principal stage for drama remains the [Lyric Theatre](/wiki/Lyric_Theatre,_Belfast "Lyric Theatre, Belfast") (1951, 2011), the largest employer of actors and other theatre professionals in the region.[[172]](#cite_note-173) At Queens University, drama students stage their productions at the [Brian Friel](/wiki/Brian_Friel "Brian Friel") Theatre, a 120-seat studio space (named after the renowned playwright).[[173]](#cite_note-174)

In November 2011, Belfast became the smallest city to host the [MTV Europe Music Awards](/wiki/2011_MTV_Europe_Music_Awards "2011 MTV Europe Music Awards").[[174]](#cite_note-175) The event was made possible by the 11,000-seat Odyssey Arena (today the [SSE Arena](/wiki/SSE_Arena,_Belfast "SSE Arena, Belfast")) which opened in 2000 at the entrance to the [Titanic Quarter](/wiki/Titanic_Quarter "Titanic Quarter")[[175]](#cite_note-176) Among other annual events, the SSE Arena hosts the [Belfast International Tattoo](/wiki/Belfast_International_Tattoo "Belfast International Tattoo") which aims to showcase [Ulster-Scots](/wiki/Ulster_Scots_people "Ulster Scots people") heritage alongside international acts, including [pipe bands](/wiki/Pipe_band "Pipe band"), [military](/wiki/Military_band "Military band") and civilian musicians, and dancers.[[176]](#cite_note-:6-177)[[177]](#cite_note-:27-178) A further large-scale venue is the [Waterfront Hall](/wiki/Waterfront_Hall "Waterfront Hall"), a multi-purpose conference and entertainment centre that first opened in 1997. The main circular Auditorium seats 2,241 and is modelled on the [Berlin Philharmonic Hall](/wiki/Berliner_Philharmonie "Berliner Philharmonie").[[178]](#cite_note-179) In 2012, the [Metropolitan Arts Centre](/wiki/Metropolitan_Arts_Centre "Metropolitan Arts Centre"), the "MAC", was opened in the [Cathedral Quarter](/wiki/Cathedral_Quarter,_Belfast "Cathedral Quarter, Belfast"), offering a performance mix of music, theatre, dance and visual art.[[179]](#cite_note-180)

The [Belfast International Tattoo](/wiki/Belfast_International_Tattoo "Belfast International Tattoo") is an annual music and dance event held at the [Odyssey Arena](/wiki/Odyssey,_Belfast "Odyssey, Belfast"), Belfast.[[180]](#cite_note-181)[[181]](#cite_note-182) Inspired by the tradition of [military tattoos](/wiki/Military_tattoo "Military tattoo"), it features performances from [pipe bands](/wiki/Pipe_band "Pipe band"), [military](/wiki/Military_band "Military band") and civilian musicians, dancers, and cultural groups from Northern Ireland and around the world.[[182]](#cite_note-183) Established in 2013, the event aims to showcase [Ulster-Scots](/wiki/Ulster_Scots_people "Ulster Scots people") heritage alongside international acts, attracting large audiences from across the region and beyond.[[176]](#cite_note-:6-177)[[177]](#cite_note-:27-178)

The city has a number of community arts, and arts education, centres, among them the [Crescent Arts Centre](/wiki/Crescent_Arts_Centre "Crescent Arts Centre")[[183]](#cite_note-184) in south Belfast, the Irish-language [Cultúrlann McAdam Ó Fiaich](/wiki/Cult%C3%BArlann_McAdam_%C3%93_Fiaich "Cultúrlann McAdam Ó Fiaich")[[184]](#cite_note-185) in west Belfast, The Duncairn[[185]](#cite_note-186) in north Belfast and, in the east of the city, EastSide Arts.[[186]](#cite_note-187)

[Féile an Phobail](/wiki/F%C3%A9ile_an_Phobail "Féile an Phobail"), a community arts organisation born out of the Internment Commemorations in the west of the city, stages one of the largest community festivals in Europe.[[187]](#cite_note-BBC-188) It has grown from its original *August Féile* on the Falls Road, to a year-round programme with a broad range of arts events, talks and discussions.[[188]](#cite_note-189)

### UNESCO City of Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: UNESCO City of Music")]

In November 2021, Belfast became the third city in the [British Isles](/wiki/British_Isles "British Isles") to be designated by [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") as [City of Music](/wiki/City_of_Music_(UNESCO) "City of Music (UNESCO)") (after Glasgow in 2008 and Liverpool in 2016) and is one of 59 cities worldwide participating in the [UNESCO Creative Cities Network](/wiki/Creative_Cities_Network "Creative Cities Network").[[189]](#cite_note-190)[[190]](#cite_note-191)

The greater part of Belfast's music scene is accommodated in the city's [pubs](/wiki/Pub "Pub") and clubs. [Irish traditional music](/wiki/Irish_traditional_music "Irish traditional music") ("trad") is a staple, and is supported, along with [Ulster-Scots](/wiki/Ulster_Scots_people "Ulster Scots people") [snare drum](/wiki/Snare_drum "Snare drum") and [pipe](/wiki/Bagpipes "Bagpipes") music, by the city's TradFest summer school.[[191]](#cite_note-192)[[192]](#cite_note-193)

Music offerings also draw on the legacy of the punk[[193]](#cite_note-194) and the underground club scene that developed during [The Troubles](/wiki/The_Troubles "The Troubles")[[194]](#cite_note-195) (associated with the groups [Stiff Little Fingers](/wiki/Stiff_Little_Fingers "Stiff Little Fingers") and [The Undertones](/wiki/The_Undertones "The Undertones"), and celebrated in the award-winning 2013 film, [Good Vibrations](/wiki/Good_Vibrations_(film) "Good Vibrations (film)")).[[195]](#cite_note-196) [Snow Patrol](/wiki/Snow_Patrol "Snow Patrol")'s frontman [Gary Lightbody](/wiki/Gary_Lightbody "Gary Lightbody") led a line up of private donors that together with public funders established the [Oh Yeah music centre](/wiki/Oh_Yeah_(music_centre) "Oh Yeah (music centre)") in 2008.[[196]](#cite_note-197) The Cathedral Quarter non-profit supports young musicians and these have engaged with a range of genres including [Alternative rock](/wiki/Alternative_rock "Alternative rock"), [Indie rock](/wiki/Indie_rock "Indie rock"), [Electronica](/wiki/Electronica "Electronica"), [Post rock](/wiki/Post_rock "Post rock"), [Post punk](/wiki/Post_punk "Post punk"), [Crossover](/wiki/Crossover_(music) "Crossover (music)"), and [Experimental rock](/wiki/Experimental_rock "Experimental rock").

Queens University hosts the [Sonic Arts Research Centre](/wiki/Sonic_Arts_Research_Centre "Sonic Arts Research Centre") (SARC), an institute for music-based practice and research. Its purpose designed building, Sonic Laboratory and multichannel studios were opened by [Karlheinz Stockhausen](/wiki/Karlheinz_Stockhausen "Karlheinz Stockhausen"), the German composer and "father of electronic music",[[197]](#cite_note-198) in 2004.[[198]](#cite_note-199)

### Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Media")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/BBC_Northern_Ireland_Belfast.jpg/250px-BBC_Northern_Ireland_Belfast.jpg)](/wiki/File:BBC_Northern_Ireland_Belfast.jpg)

[Broadcasting House, Belfast](/wiki/Broadcasting_House,_Belfast "Broadcasting House, Belfast"), headquarters of the BBC in Northern Ireland

Belfast is the home of the *[Belfast Telegraph](/wiki/Belfast_Telegraph "Belfast Telegraph")*, *[Irish News](/wiki/Irish_News "Irish News")* and, first printed in 1737,*[The News Letter](/wiki/The_News_Letter "The News Letter")*, the oldest [English-language](/wiki/English_language "English language") daily newspaper in the world still in publication.[[199]](#cite_note-200)[[200]](#cite_note-201)

The city is the headquarters of [BBC Northern Ireland](/wiki/BBC_Northern_Ireland "BBC Northern Ireland"), and [ITV](/wiki/ITV_(TV_network) "ITV (TV network)") station [UTV](/wiki/Ulster_Television "Ulster Television"). The Irish public service broadcaster, [RTÉ](/wiki/Raidi%C3%B3_Teilif%C3%ADs_%C3%89ireann "Raidió Teilifís Éireann") has a studio in the city.[[201]](#cite_note-202) The national radio station is [BBC Radio Ulster](/wiki/BBC_Radio_Ulster "BBC Radio Ulster") with commercial radio stations such as [Q Radio](/wiki/Q_Radio_Belfast "Q Radio Belfast"), [U105](/wiki/U105 "U105"), [Blast 106](/wiki/Blast_106 "Blast 106") and Irish-language station [Raidió Fáilte](/wiki/Raidi%C3%B3_F%C3%A1ilte "Raidió Fáilte"). [Queen's Radio](/wiki/Queen%27s_Radio "Queen's Radio"), a student-run radio station broadcasts from [Queen's University Students' Union](/wiki/Queen%27s_University_Belfast_Students%27_Union "Queen's University Belfast Students' Union").

One of Northern Ireland's two community TV stations, [NvTv](/wiki/NvTv "NvTv"), is based in the [Cathedral Quarter](/wiki/Cathedral_Quarter,_Belfast "Cathedral Quarter, Belfast") of the city. Broadcasting only over the Internet is Homely Planet, the Cultural Radio Station for Northern Ireland, supporting community relations.[[202]](#cite_note-203)

### Parades

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Parades")]

Since the lifting in 1872 of a twenty-year [party processions ban](/wiki/Party_Processions_Act_1850 "Party Processions Act 1850"), [Orange](/wiki/Orange_Order "Orange Order") parades in celebration of "[the Twelfth](/wiki/The_Twelfth "The Twelfth")" [of July] and the bonfires of the previous evening, the eleventh, have been a fixed fixture of the Belfast calendar.[[203]](#cite_note-Bryan2009-204) On what became a public holiday in 1926,[[204]](#cite_note-205) Belfast and guest Orange lodges (from both across Ulster and Scotland) with their pipe, flute, drum and accordion bands muster at Carlisle Circus, and parade through the city centre past the City Hall and out the Lisburn Road to a gathering in "the field" at Barnett Demesne.[[205]](#cite_note-206) While some local feeder and return marches have a history of sectarian disturbance, in recent years, events have generally passed off without serious incident.[[206]](#cite_note-207) The tradition is documented and celebrated in the Museum of Orange Heritage on the Cregagh Road in East Belfast.[[125]](#cite_note-:24-126)

What is sometimes referred to as the Catholic equivalent of the Orangemen,[[207]](#cite_note-208) the much smaller [Ancient Order of Hibernians](/wiki/Ancient_Order_of_Hibernians "Ancient Order of Hibernians"), confines its parades to nationalist areas in west and north Belfast,[[208]](#cite_note-209) as do republicans commemorating the [Easter Rising](/wiki/Easter_Rising "Easter Rising").[[209]](#cite_note-210) In August 1993, in a break with a history of nationalist exclusion from the city centre, a parade marking the [introduction of internment](/wiki/Operation_Demetrius "Operation Demetrius") in the 1971 proceeded up [Royal Avenue](/wiki/Royal_Avenue,_Belfast "Royal Avenue, Belfast") toward the City Hall, where it was addressed by Sinn Féin president, [Gerry Adams](/wiki/Gerry_Adams "Gerry Adams"), in front of the statue of [Queen Victoria](/wiki/Queen_Victoria "Queen Victoria").[[203]](#cite_note-Bryan2009-204)

Since 1998, the Belfast City Council has funded a city-centre [St. Patrick's Day](/wiki/Saint_Patrick%27s_Day "Saint Patrick's Day") (17 March) celebration. It is organised by [Féile an Phobail](/wiki/F%C3%A9ile_an_Phobail "Féile an Phobail") as a "carnival" complete with a parade featuring dancers, circus entertainers, floats, and giant puppets. Critical of what they perceive as an evolving nationalist festival, unionists on the City Council observe that "a lot of the Protestant Unionist Loyalist (PUL) community will stay away from the city centre on St Patrick's Day, the same as some stay away on the Twelfth of July".[[210]](#cite_note-211)

In 1991, Belfast hosted its first [gay pride](/wiki/Gay_pride "Gay pride") event. Belfast Pride, culminating in a city-centre parade at the end of July, is now one of the biggest annual festivals in the city and, according to its organisers, the largest [LGBT](/wiki/LGBT "LGBT")+ festival in Ireland.[[211]](#cite_note-212)[[212]](#cite_note-213)

The [Irish Congress of Trade Unions](/wiki/Irish_Congress_of_Trade_Unions "Irish Congress of Trade Unions") organises an annual city-centre May Day march and rally.[[213]](#cite_note-214) The [International Workers Day](/wiki/International_Workers%27_Day "International Workers' Day") has been a public holiday since 1978.[[214]](#cite_note-215)

In 2021, there were 345,418 residents within the expanded 2015 Belfast local government boundary[[64]](#cite_note-:2-65) and 634,600 in the [Belfast Metropolitan Area](/wiki/Belfast_Metropolitan_Area "Belfast Metropolitan Area"),[[223]](#cite_note-224) approximately one third of Northern Ireland's 1.9 million population.

As with many cities, Belfast's inner city is currently characterised by the elderly, students and single young people, while families tend to live on the periphery. Socio-economic areas radiate out from the [Central Business District](/wiki/Belfast_City_Centre "Belfast City Centre"), with a pronounced wedge of affluence extending out the Malone Road and Upper Malone Road to the south.[[224]](#cite_note-Geography_in_Action_Urban_Growth-225) Deprivation levels are notable in the inner parts of the north and the west of the city. The areas around the [Falls Road](/wiki/Falls_Road,_Belfast "Falls Road, Belfast"), [Ardoyne](/wiki/Ardoyne "Ardoyne") and [New Lodge](/wiki/New_Lodge,_Belfast "New Lodge, Belfast") (Catholic nationalist) and the [Shankill Road](/wiki/Shankill_Road "Shankill Road") (Protestant loyalist) experience some of the highest levels of social deprivation including higher levels of ill health and poor access to services. These areas remain firmly segregated, with 80 to 90 percent of residents being of the one religious designation.[[225]](#cite_note-226)[[226]](#cite_note-227)
