Consistent with the trend across all of Northern Ireland, the Protestant population within the city has been in decline, while the non-religious, other religious and Catholic population has risen. The 2021 census recorded the following: 43% of residents as Catholic, 12% as Presbyterian, 8% as Church of Ireland, 3% as Methodist, 6% as belonging to other Christian denominations, 3% to other religions and 24% as having either no religion or no declared religion.[[155]](#cite_note-:02-156)

In terms of community background, 47.93% were deemed to belong to, or to have been brought up in, the Catholic faith and 36.45% in a Protestant or other Christian-related denomination.[[227]](#cite_note-228) The comparable figures in 2011 were 48.60% Catholic and 42.28% Protestant or other Christian-related denomination.[[228]](#cite_note-229)

With respondents free to indicate more than one national identity, in 2021 the largest national identity group was "Irish only" with 35% of the population, followed by "British only" 27%, "Northern Irish only" 17%, "British and Northern Irish only" 7%, "Irish and Northern Irish only" 2%, "British, Irish and Northern Irish only" 2%, British and Irish less than 1% and Other identities with 10%.[[155]](#cite_note-:02-156)

Insofar as the city's two indigenous minority languages (Irish and [Ulster Scots](/wiki/Ulster_Scots_dialect "Ulster Scots dialect")) are concerned, figures are made available from the decennial UK census. On census day, 21 March 2021, 14.93% (43,798) in Belfast claimed to have some knowledge of the Irish language, whilst 5.21% (15,294) claimed to be able to speak, read, write and understand spoken Irish.[[229]](#cite_note-230) 3.74% (10,963) of residents claimed to use Irish daily and 0.75% (2,192) claimed Irish is their main language.[[230]](#cite_note-231)[[231]](#cite_note-232) 7.17% (21,025) of people in the city claimed to have some knowledge of Ulster Scots, whilst 0.75% (2,207) claimed to be able to speak, read, write and understand spoken Ulster Scots.[[232]](#cite_note-233) 0.83% (2,430) claimed to use Ulster Scots daily.[[233]](#cite_note-234)

From the mid to late 19th century, there was a community [of central European Jews](/wiki/Belfast_Jewish_Community "Belfast Jewish Community")[[234]](#cite_note-235) (among its distinguished members, two-time Lord Mayor [Otto Jaffe](/wiki/Otto_Jaffe "Otto Jaffe"))[[235]](#cite_note-236) and of Italians[[236]](#cite_note-237) in Belfast. Today, the largest immigrant groups are Poles, Chinese and Indians. The 2011 census figures recorded a total non-white population of 10,219 or 3.3%, while 18,420 or 6.6% of the population were born outside the UK and Ireland. Almost half of those born outside the British Isles lived in [south Belfast](/wiki/Belfast_South_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Belfast South (UK Parliament constituency)"), where they comprised 9.5% of the population.[[237]](#cite_note-QS208NI%2520(a)-238) The majority of the estimated 5,000 Muslims[[238]](#cite_note-239) and 200 [Hindu](/wiki/Hindu "Hindu") families[[239]](#cite_note-240) living in Northern Ireland resided in the [Greater Belfast](/wiki/Greater_Belfast "Greater Belfast") area. In the 2021 census the percentage of the city's residents born outside the United Kingdom had risen to 9.8.[[82]](#cite_note-:5-83)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Economy")]

Main article: [Economy of Belfast](/wiki/Economy_of_Belfast "Economy of Belfast")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Harlandandwolffcranes.JPG/250px-Harlandandwolffcranes.JPG)](/wiki/File:Harlandandwolffcranes.JPG)

[Samson and Goliath](/wiki/Samson_and_Goliath_(cranes) "Samson and Goliath (cranes)"), Harland & Wolff's gantry cranes

### Employment profile

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Employment profile")]

Services (including retail, health, professional & scientific) account for three quarters of jobs in the Belfast. Only 6% remain in manufacturing. The balance is in distribution and construction.[[240]](#cite_note-241) In recent years, unemployment has been comparatively low (under 3% in the summer of 2023) for the UK. On the other hand, Belfast has a high rate of people economically inactive (close to 30%).[[241]](#cite_note-242) It is a group, encompassing homemakers, full-time carers, students and retirees,[[242]](#cite_note-243) that in Belfast has been swollen by the exceptionally large proportion of the population (27%) with [long-term health problems or disabilities](/wiki/Disability_in_Northern_Ireland "Disability in Northern Ireland")[[243]](#cite_note-244) (and who, in Northern Ireland generally, are less likely to be employed than in other UK regions).[[244]](#cite_note-245)

An early report on the post-Belfast Agreement prospects for the city economy underscored another distinctive feature of its working-age population. While it appeared well qualified, with 24 per cent educated to degree level, at "the other end of the educational spectrum", 26 per cent had no qualifications at all, a much higher share than in English cities.[[25]](#cite_note-:26-26): 251

### Shipbuilding and engineering

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Shipbuilding and engineering")]

Of Belfast's Victorian-era industry, little remains. The last working linen factory—Copeland Linens Limited, based in the Shankill area—closed in 2013.[[245]](#cite_note-246) In recent years [Harland & Wolff](/wiki/Harland_%26_Wolff "Harland & Wolff"), which at peak production in the Second World War had employed around 35,000 people, has had a workforce of no more than two or three hundred refurbishing oil rigs and fabricating off-shore wind turbines. A £1.6 billion [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") contract has offered the yard a new lease, returning it to shipbuilding in 2025,[[31]](#cite_note-:3-32): 261–262[[246]](#cite_note-247) a prospect secured by the purchase of insolvent yard by Spain's state-owned shipbuilder, [Navantia](/wiki/Navantia "Navantia").[[247]](#cite_note-248)

In 1936, Short & Harland Ltd, a joint venture of [Short Brothers](/wiki/Short_Brothers "Short Brothers") and Harland & Wolff, began the manufacture of aircraft in the docks area. In 1989, the British government, which had nationalised the company during the Second World War, sold it to the Canadian aerospace company [Bombardier](/wiki/Bombardier_Inc. "Bombardier Inc."). In 2020, it was sold on to the American aerostructure company [Spirit AeroSystems](/wiki/Spirit_AeroSystems "Spirit AeroSystems").[[248]](#cite_note-spirt-completes-249) Producing aircraft components, it remains the largest manufacturing concern in Northern Ireland.[[249]](#cite_note-250)

Originating in the [Short Brothers](/wiki/Short_Brothers "Short Brothers")' missile division, since 2001 the [Thales Group](/wiki/Thales_Group "Thales Group")[[250]](#cite_note-251) (owned by the French defense contractor [Thales Air Defence](/wiki/Thales_Air_Defence "Thales Air Defence") Limited)[[251]](#cite_note-252) has been producing [short range air defence](/wiki/Short_range_air_defence "Short range air defence") and [anti-tank](/wiki/Anti-tank_guided_missile "Anti-tank guided missile") [missiles](/wiki/Missile "Missile")[[252]](#cite_note-253) including the [NLAW](/wiki/NLAW "NLAW") shoulder-launched system[[253]](#cite_note-254) and, from 2025, lightweight multirole missiles (LMMs), deployed in the [Russo-Ukrainian war](/wiki/Russo-Ukrainian_war_(2022%E2%80%93present) "Russo-Ukrainian war (2022–present)") by [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine").[[254]](#cite_note-255)

In 2025, the [Belfast Port and Harbour Estate](/wiki/Belfast_Harbour "Belfast Harbour") announced capital projects amounting to than £313m over five years. These include a new deepwater quay to accommodate, in addition to larger cruise liners, an expansion the port’s capacity for offshore wind turbine assembly and installation.[[255]](#cite_note-256)

### Fintech and cybersecurity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Fintech and cybersecurity")]

From the 1990s, Belfast established itself as a significant location for call centres and for other back-office services.[[256]](#cite_note-The_Times-257) Attracting U.S. operators such as [Citi](/wiki/Citigroup "Citigroup"), [Allstate](/wiki/Allstate "Allstate"), [Liberty Mutual](/wiki/Liberty_Mutual "Liberty Mutual"), [Aflac](/wiki/Aflac "Aflac") and [FD Technologies (Kx Systems)](/wiki/Kx_Systems "Kx Systems")[[257]](#cite_note-258) and most recently [Bank of America](/wiki/Bank_of_America "Bank of America"),[[258]](#cite_note-259) it has since been identified by the [UK Treasury](/wiki/HM_Treasury "HM Treasury") as "key [fintech](/wiki/Fintech "Fintech") [financial technology] hub".[[259]](#cite_note-260) Fintech's key areas (its "ABCD") are [artificial intelligence](/wiki/Artificial_intelligence "Artificial intelligence"), [blockchain](/wiki/Blockchain "Blockchain"), [cloud computing](/wiki/Cloud_computing "Cloud computing"), and [big data](/wiki/Big_data "Big data").[[260]](#cite_note-LaiLiao2020-261)

The sector's principal constraint, cyber security, has been addressed since 2004 by the Queens University Institute of Electronics, Communications and Information Technology (IECIT), and its [Centre for Secure Information Technologies](/wiki/Centre_for_Secure_Information_Technologies "Centre for Secure Information Technologies") (CSIT).[[261]](#cite_note-262) The IECIT is the anchor tenant at [Catalyst (science park)](/wiki/Catalyst_(science_park) "Catalyst (science park)")[[262]](#cite_note-263) in the Titanic Quarter, which hosts a cluster of cyber-security companies.[[263]](#cite_note-264)

### Film

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Film")]

Between 2018 and 2023, film and television production based largely in Belfast, and occupying significant new studio capacity in the ports area, contributed £330m to Northern Ireland's economy.[[264]](#cite_note-Belvins2024-265) There are two 8-acre media complexes (serviced by the adjacent [City Airport](/wiki/George_Best_Belfast_City_Airport "George Best Belfast City Airport")): the Titanic Studios on Queen's Island (the [Titanic Quarter](/wiki/Titanic_Quarter "Titanic Quarter")) and across the Victoria Channel in [Giant's Park](/wiki/Giant%27s_Park "Giant's Park") on the Lough's north foreshore, the Belfast Harbour Studios.[[265]](#cite_note-:19-266) Together they offer 226,000 ft2 of studio space, plus offices and workshops,[[266]](#cite_note-Film_&_Television_Studios-267) and have attracted U.S. production companies such as [Amazon](/wiki/Amazon_Prime_Video "Amazon Prime Video"), [HBO](/wiki/HBO "HBO") (including all eight series of its fantasy drama *[Game of Thrones](/wiki/Game_of_Thrones "Game of Thrones")*), [Paramount](/wiki/Paramount_Pictures "Paramount Pictures"), [Playtone](/wiki/Universal_Pictures "Universal Pictures"), [Universal](/wiki/Universal_Pictures "Universal Pictures"), and [Warner Bros](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Pictures "Warner Bros. Pictures").[[162]](#cite_note-:15-163)[[265]](#cite_note-:19-266)

At the beginning of 2024, [Ulster University](/wiki/Ulster_University "Ulster University"), in partnership with [Belfast Harbour](/wiki/Belfast_Harbour "Belfast Harbour") and supported by [Northern Ireland Screen](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Screen "Northern Ireland Screen"), announced an £72m investment to add to the complex a new virtual production, research and development, facility, Studio Ulster.[[267]](#cite_note-268)[[264]](#cite_note-Belvins2024-265) Additional studio space is available at Loop Studios (formerly Britvic) on the Castlereagh Road in East Belfast.[[266]](#cite_note-Film_&_Television_Studios-267)[[268]](#cite_note-269)

In May 2025, Belfast was named "City of Film’"at the Global Production Awards ceremony held during the [Cannes Film Festival](/wiki/Cannes_Film_Festival "Cannes Film Festival") in France.[[269]](#cite_note-270)

### Tourism and hospitality

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Tourism and hospitality")]

Northern Ireland's [peace dividend](/wiki/Peace_dividend "Peace dividend") since the 1990s, which includes a marked increase in [inward investment](/wiki/Inward_investment "Inward investment"),[[270]](#cite_note-271)[[271]](#cite_note-272) has contributed to a large-scale redevelopment of the city centre. Significant projects included [Victoria Square](/wiki/Victoria_Square,_Belfast "Victoria Square, Belfast"), the [Cathedral Quarter](/wiki/Cathedral_Quarter,_Belfast "Cathedral Quarter, Belfast"), [Laganside](/wiki/Laganside "Laganside") with the [Odyssey](/wiki/Odyssey_(Belfast) "Odyssey (Belfast)") complex and the landmark [Waterfront Hall](/wiki/Waterfront_Hall "Waterfront Hall"), the new [Titanic Quarter](/wiki/Titanic_Quarter,_Belfast "Titanic Quarter, Belfast") with its [*Titanic* Belfast](/wiki/Titanic_Belfast "Titanic Belfast") visitor attraction, and the development of the original Short's harbour airfield as [George Best Belfast City Airport](/wiki/George_Best_Belfast_City_Airport "George Best Belfast City Airport").
These developments reflect a boom in tourism (32 million visitors between 2011 and 2018),[[31]](#cite_note-:3-32): 179 and related hotel construction.

This has included an entirely new phenomenon for Belfast: in 1996, the port received its first cruise ship.[[272]](#cite_note-273) In 2023, Belfast welcomed 153 calls, 8% up from the pre-[pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic") record set in 2019. Ships from 32 different countries landed 320,000 passengers.[[273]](#cite_note-274) By 2028, there is to be a new a £90 million deep-water quay capable of accommodating the world’s largest cruise liners.[[274]](#cite_note-275)

Belfast has also seen growth of "conflict tourism".[[31]](#cite_note-:3-32): 186–191 To the dismay of some, "tourists take photos of the division lines that are not consigned to history, but are a part of living Belfast: children play football against the walls that tourists flock to. The places and the people themselves have become a spectacle, an attraction."[[275]](#cite_note-276) Tourist bosses and guides, however, are satisfied that the greater draw is city's other "must-see attractions",[[276]](#cite_note-277) and its "convivial food and nightlife scene".[[277]](#cite_note-278)

### EU/GB Trade

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: EU/GB Trade")]

[Invest NI](/wiki/Invest_Northern_Ireland "Invest Northern Ireland"), Northern Ireland's economic development agency is pitching Belfast and its hinterland to foreign investors as "only region in the world able to trade goods freely with both [GB](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain") and EU markets".[[278]](#cite_note-279) This follows the 2020 [Northern Ireland Protocol](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Protocol "Northern Ireland Protocol") and the 2023 [Windsor Framework](/wiki/Windsor_Framework "Windsor Framework"), agreements between the British government and [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"), whereby, post-Brexit, Northern Ireland would effectively remain within the [European Single Market](/wiki/European_single_market "European single market") for goods while, in principle, retaining unfettered access to the British domestic market. Despite the DUP's derailment of devolved government in protest, local business leaders largely welcomed the new trade regime, hailing the promise of dual EU-GB access as a critical opportunity.[[279]](#cite_note-280)[[280]](#cite_note-281)

In February 2024, the DUP consented to a return of the devolved Assembly and Executive on the understanding that neither the EU nor the British government would defend the integrity of their respective internal markets by conducting *routine* checks on the bulk of goods passing through Belfast, or other Northern Ireland, ports.[[281]](#cite_note-282)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Education")]

See also: [List of primary schools in Belfast](/wiki/List_of_primary_schools_in_Belfast "List of primary schools in Belfast"), [List of secondary schools in Belfast](/wiki/List_of_secondary_schools_in_Belfast "List of secondary schools in Belfast"), and [List of grammar schools in Belfast](/wiki/List_of_grammar_schools_in_Belfast "List of grammar schools in Belfast")

### Primary and secondary education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Primary and secondary education")]

Children from Catholic and Protestant homes in Belfast are taught, for the most part, separately on a pattern that, by the mid-nineteenth century, had been established throughout Ireland.[[282]](#cite_note-283) Primary and secondary education is divided between (Catholic) Maintained Schools and (non-Catholic/ "Protestant") Controlled Schools.[[283]](#cite_note-:7-284) They are bound by the same curriculum, but their teaching staff are trained separately (in the university colleges of [St Mary's](/wiki/St_Mary%27s_University_College,_Belfast "St Mary's University College, Belfast") and [Stranmillis](/wiki/Stranmillis_University_College "Stranmillis University College")).[[284]](#cite_note-285)[[285]](#cite_note-:23-286): 200–202

Since the 1980s, two smaller school sectors have emerged: grant-maintained [Integrated schools](/wiki/Integrated_education_in_Northern_Ireland "Integrated education in Northern Ireland"), which by design bring together children and staff from both communities, and [Irish language medium schools](/wiki/Comhairle_na_Gaelscola%C3%ADochta "Comhairle na Gaelscolaíochta")[[283]](#cite_note-:7-284)

The [Belfast [later *Royal* Belfast] Academical Institution](/wiki/Royal_Belfast_Academical_Institution "Royal Belfast Academical Institution"), opened its doors in 1810 with the intention, in the words of its founder, former [United Irishman](/wiki/Society_of_United_Irishmen "Society of United Irishmen") [William Drennan](/wiki/William_Drennan "William Drennan"), of being "perfectly unbiased by religious distinctions".[[286]](#cite_note-287) The principle was not embraced by the town's middle-classes. In practice, "Inst" provided a [grammar education](/wiki/Grammar_school "Grammar school") to the town's Presbyterian families while Anglicans favoured the older [Royal Belfast Academy](/wiki/Belfast_Royal_Academy "Belfast Royal Academy") (1785); Catholics, [St Malachy's](/wiki/St_Malachy%27s_College "St Malachy's College") [diocesan](/wiki/Diocese "Diocese") college (1833) and [Wesleyans](/wiki/Wesleyans "Wesleyans"), [Methodist College Belfast](/wiki/Methodist_College_Belfast "Methodist College Belfast") (1865).

Denominational lines have since blurred, with Catholics in particular moving into the controlled grammars.[[287]](#cite_note-288) But the presence of 18 [selective grammar schools in Belfast](/wiki/Category:Grammar_schools_in_Belfast "Category:Grammar schools in Belfast") is a further feature of post-primary education in Belfast that distinguishes it from that of comparable cities in Great Britain where academic selection was abandoned in the 1960s and 70s.[[288]](#cite_note-289) Partly prompted by the [COVID](/wiki/COVID-19 "COVID-19") disruption of external testing in 2021/22,[[289]](#cite_note-290) some the city's grammars have begun to review and amend the practice. It is not clear that this will be on terms that reduce the degree of social segregation they have represented within the system.[[290]](#cite_note-291)

In 2006, the [Belfast Education and Library Board](/wiki/Belfast_Education_and_Library_Board "Belfast Education and Library Board") became part of the consolidated [Education Authority](/wiki/Education_Authority "Education Authority") for Northern Ireland. In Belfast, the Authority has responsibility for 156 primary,[[291]](#cite_note-292) and 48 [secondary](/wiki/List_of_secondary_schools_in_Belfast "List of secondary schools in Belfast") schools (including the 18 grammars).[[292]](#cite_note-293) The system is marked by stark inequalities in outcome.[[293]](#cite_note-294) Around 30% of school leavers in the city do not attain 5 [GCSEs](/wiki/GCSE "GCSE"), A\* - C (including Maths and English). For those in receipt of free school meals, the figure rises to over 50%.[[294]](#cite_note-295)

### Further and Higher education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Further and Higher education")]

[Belfast Metropolitan College](/wiki/Belfast_Metropolitan_College "Belfast Metropolitan College") ("Belfast Met") is a [further education](/wiki/Further_education "Further education") college with three main campuses around the city, including several smaller buildings. Formerly known as [Belfast Institute of Further and Higher Education](/wiki/Belfast_Institute_of_Further_and_Higher_Education "Belfast Institute of Further and Higher Education"), it specialises in [vocational education](/wiki/Vocational_education "Vocational education"). The college has over 53,000 students enrolled on full-time and part-time courses, making it one of the largest further education colleges in the UK and the largest in the island of Ireland.[[295]](#cite_note-296)

Belfast has two universities. [Queen's University Belfast](/wiki/Queen%27s_University_Belfast "Queen's University Belfast") was founded as a college in 1845. In 1908, the Catholic bishops lifted their ban on attendance and Queen's was granted university status.[[285]](#cite_note-:23-286): 164, 166 It is a member of the [Russell Group](/wiki/Russell_Group "Russell Group"), an association of 24 leading research-intensive universities in the UK,[[296]](#cite_note-297) and is one of the largest universities in the UK with over 25,000 students – among them over 4,000 international students.[[297]](#cite_note-298)

[Ulster University](/wiki/Ulster_University "Ulster University"), created in its current form in 1984, is a multi-centre university with a campus on the edge of the [Cathedral Quarter](/wiki/Cathedral_Quarter,_Belfast "Cathedral Quarter, Belfast") of Belfast. Since 2021, this original "Arts College" campus has undergone a £1.4bn expansion to accommodate offerings across all departments. The project promises to bring 15,500 staff and students into the city, and to generate 5,000 new jobs.[[298]](#cite_note-299)[[145]](#cite_note-Ulster280723-146)

## Governance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Governance")]

Belfast was granted [borough status](/wiki/Borough_status_in_the_United_Kingdom "Borough status in the United Kingdom") by [James VI and I](/wiki/James_VI_and_I "James VI and I") in 1613 and official [city status](/wiki/City_status_in_the_United_Kingdom "City status in the United Kingdom") by [Queen Victoria](/wiki/Queen_Victoria "Queen Victoria") in 1888.[[299]](#cite_note-300) Since 1973 it has been a [local government district](/wiki/Local_Government_(Boundaries)_Act_(Northern_Ireland)_1971 "Local Government (Boundaries) Act (Northern Ireland) 1971") under local administration by [Belfast City Council](/wiki/Belfast_City_Council "Belfast City Council").[[300]](#cite_note-301)

Belfast has been represented in the [British House of Commons](/wiki/British_House_of_Commons "British House of Commons") since 1801, and in [Northern Ireland Assembly](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Assembly "Northern Ireland Assembly"), as presently constituted, since 1998.

### Local government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Local government")]

Further information: [Belfast City Council](/wiki/Belfast_City_Council "Belfast City Council")

Belfast City Council is responsible for a range of powers and services, including land-use and community planning, parks and recreation, building control, arts and cultural heritage.[[301]](#cite_note-302) The city's principal offices are those of the [Lord Mayor of Belfast](/wiki/Lord_Mayor_of_Belfast "Lord Mayor of Belfast"), Deputy Lord Mayor and [High Sheriff](/wiki/High_Sheriff "High Sheriff"). Like other elected positions within the Council such as Committee chairs, these are filled since 1998 using the [D'Hondt system](/wiki/D%27Hondt_system "D'Hondt system") so that in recent years the position has rotated between councillors from the three largest factions, [Sinn Féin](/wiki/Sinn_F%C3%A9in "Sinn Féin"), the [Democratic Unionist Party](/wiki/Democratic_Unionist_Party "Democratic Unionist Party") and the [Alliance Party](/wiki/Alliance_Party_of_Northern_Ireland "Alliance Party of Northern Ireland").

The first Lord Mayor of Belfast in 1892, [Daniel Dixon](/wiki/Sir_Daniel_Dixon,_1st_Baronet "Sir Daniel Dixon, 1st Baronet"), like [every mayor but one](/wiki/List_of_mayors_of_Belfast "List of mayors of Belfast") until 1997 (Alliance in 1979), was a [unionist](/wiki/Unionism_in_Ireland "Unionism in Ireland").[[302]](#cite_note-Belfast_City_Council_–_Lord_Mayor-303) The first [nationalist](/wiki/Irish_nationalism "Irish nationalism") Lord Mayor of Belfast was [Alban Maginness](/wiki/Alban_Maginness "Alban Maginness") of the [Social Democratic and Labour Party](/wiki/Social_Democratic_and_Labour_Party "Social Democratic and Labour Party") (SDLP) in 1997. The current [Lord Mayor](/wiki/Lord_Mayor "Lord Mayor") is Councillor [Tracy Kelly](/wiki/Tracy_Kelly "Tracy Kelly") of the DUP, who has been in the position of Lord Mayor since 2 June 2025. Her duties include presiding over meetings of the council, receiving distinguished visitors to the city, representing and promoting the city on the national and international stage.[[302]](#cite_note-Belfast_City_Council_–_Lord_Mayor-303)

In 1997, unionists lost overall control of Belfast City Council for the first time in its history, with the Alliance Party holding the balance of power. In 2023, unionists retained just 17 of 60 seats on the council, leaving nationalists (Sinn Féin and the SDLP) just 4 seats short of a majority.[[303]](#cite_note-304) In addition to the 11 Alliance members there are four other councillors, 3 [Green](/wiki/Green_Party_Northern_Ireland "Green Party Northern Ireland") and 1 [People Before Profit (PBP)](/wiki/People_Before_Profit "People Before Profit"), who refuse a nationalist/unionist designation.

### Northern Ireland Assembly and Westminster elections

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Northern Ireland Assembly and Westminster elections")]

Further information: [Northern Ireland Assembly](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Assembly "Northern Ireland Assembly") and [Parliament of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom")

See also: [Belfast (Northern Ireland Parliament constituencies)](/wiki/Belfast_(Northern_Ireland_Parliament_constituencies) "Belfast (Northern Ireland Parliament constituencies)") and [Belfast (UK Parliament constituency)](/wiki/Belfast_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Belfast (UK Parliament constituency)")

As Northern Ireland's capital city, Belfast is host to the [Northern Ireland Assembly](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Assembly "Northern Ireland Assembly") at [Stormont](/wiki/Parliament_Buildings_(Northern_Ireland) "Parliament Buildings (Northern Ireland)"), the site of the [devolved](/wiki/Devolution#Northern_Ireland "Devolution") legislature for Northern Ireland. Belfast is divided into four [Northern Ireland Assembly](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Assembly "Northern Ireland Assembly") and [UK parliamentary](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom") constituencies: [Belfast North](/wiki/Belfast_North_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Belfast North (UK Parliament constituency)"), [Belfast West](/wiki/Belfast_West_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Belfast West (UK Parliament constituency)"), [Belfast South and Mid Down](/wiki/Belfast_South_and_Mid_Down "Belfast South and Mid Down") and [Belfast East](/wiki/Belfast_East_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Belfast East (UK Parliament constituency)"). All four extend beyond the city boundaries to include parts of [Antrim and Newtownabbey](/wiki/Antrim_and_Newtownabbey_Borough_Council "Antrim and Newtownabbey Borough Council") and [Lisburn and Castlereagh](/wiki/Lisburn_and_Castlereagh "Lisburn and Castlereagh") districts. In [United Kingdom elections](/wiki/Elections_in_the_United_Kingdom "Elections in the United Kingdom"), each constituency returns one [MP](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament_(United_Kingdom) "Member of Parliament (United Kingdom)"), on a "[first past the post](/wiki/First-past-the-post_voting "First-past-the-post voting")" basis to Westminster. In NI Assembly elections each returns, on the basis of [proportional representation](/wiki/Proportional_representation "Proportional representation"), five [MLAs](/wiki/Member_of_the_Legislative_Assembly_(Northern_Ireland) "Member of the Legislative Assembly (Northern Ireland)") to [Stormont](/wiki/Parliament_Buildings_(Northern_Ireland) "Parliament Buildings (Northern Ireland)").

In the [Northern Ireland Assembly Elections in 2022](/wiki/2022_Northern_Ireland_Assembly_election "2022 Northern Ireland Assembly election"), Belfast elected 7 [Sinn Féin](/wiki/Sinn_F%C3%A9in "Sinn Féin"), 5 [DUP](/wiki/Democratic_Unionist_Party "Democratic Unionist Party"), 5 [Alliance Party](/wiki/Alliance_Party_of_Northern_Ireland "Alliance Party of Northern Ireland"), 1 [SDLP](/wiki/Social_Democratic_and_Labour_Party "Social Democratic and Labour Party"), 1 [UUP](/wiki/Ulster_Unionist_Party "Ulster Unionist Party") and 1 [PBP](/wiki/People_Before_Profit_Alliance "People Before Profit Alliance") MLAs.[[304]](#cite_note-305) In the [2017 UK general election](/wiki/2017_United_Kingdom_general_election "2017 United Kingdom general election"), the DUP won all but the Sinn Féin stronghold of Belfast West. In the [2019](/wiki/2019_UK_General_Election "2019 UK General Election") and [2024 UK general elections](/wiki/2024_United_Kingdom_general_election "2024 United Kingdom general election"), they retained only Belfast East, losing Belfast North to Sinn Féin and Belfast South to the SDLP.

## Infrastructure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Infrastructure")]

### Hospitals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Hospitals")]

The [Belfast Health & Social Care Trust](/wiki/Belfast_Health_and_Social_Care_Trust "Belfast Health and Social Care Trust") is one of five trusts that were created on 1 April 2007 by the [Department of Health](/wiki/Department_of_Health,_Social_Services_and_Public_Safety "Department of Health, Social Services and Public Safety"). Belfast contains most of Northern Ireland's regional specialist centres.[[305]](#cite_note-306)

The Royal Hospitals site in west Belfast (junction of Grosvenor and Falls roads) contains two hospitals. The [Royal Victoria Hospital](/wiki/Royal_Victoria_Hospital,_Belfast "Royal Victoria Hospital, Belfast") (its origins in a number of successive institutions, beginning in 1797 with The Belfast Fever Hospital)[[306]](#cite_note-307) provides both local and regional services. Specialist services include cardiac surgery, critical care and the Regional Trauma Centre.[[307]](#cite_note-308) The Children's Hospital ([Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children](/wiki/Royal_Belfast_Hospital_for_Sick_Children "Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children")) provides general hospital care for children in Belfast and provides most of the paediatric regional specialities.[[308]](#cite_note-309)

The [Belfast City Hospital](/wiki/Belfast_City_Hospital "Belfast City Hospital") (evolved from the 1841 [Belfast Union Workhouse](/wiki/Belfast_Union_Workhouse "Belfast Union Workhouse") and infirmary)[[309]](#cite_note-310) on the Lisburn Road is the regional specialist centre for haematology and is home to a major cancer centre.[[310]](#cite_note-311) The Mary G McGeown Regional Nephrology Unit at the [City Hospital](/wiki/Belfast_City_Hospital "Belfast City Hospital") is the kidney transplant centre and provides regional renal services for Northern Ireland.[[311]](#cite_note-312)
[Musgrave Park Hospital](/wiki/Musgrave_Park_Hospital "Musgrave Park Hospital") (1920) in south Belfast specialises in [orthopaedics](/wiki/Orthopedic_surgery "Orthopedic surgery"), [rheumatology](/wiki/Rheumatology "Rheumatology"), [sports medicine](/wiki/Sports_medicine "Sports medicine") and [rehabilitation](/wiki/Rehabilitation_psychology "Rehabilitation psychology"). It is home to Northern Ireland's first Acquired Brain Injury Unit.[[312]](#cite_note-313)

The [Mater Hospital](/wiki/Mater_Infirmorum_Hospital "Mater Infirmorum Hospital") (founded in 1883 by the [Sisters of Mercy](/wiki/Sisters_of_Mercy "Sisters of Mercy"))[[313]](#cite_note-314) on the Crumlin Road provides a wide range of services, including acute inpatient, emergency and maternity services, to north Belfast and the surrounding areas.[[314]](#cite_note-315)

The [Ulster Hospital](/wiki/Ulster_Hospital "Ulster Hospital"), Upper Newtownards Road, [Dundonald](/wiki/Dundonald,_County_Down "Dundonald, County Down"), on the eastern edge of the city, first founded as the Ulster Hospital for Women and Sick Children in 1872,[[315]](#cite_note-journal-316) is the major acute hospital for the [South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust](/wiki/South_Eastern_Health_and_Social_Care_Trust "South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust"). It delivers a full range of outpatient, inpatient and daycare medical and surgical services.[[316]](#cite_note-317)

### Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Transport")]

Main article: [Transport in Belfast](/wiki/Transport_in_Belfast "Transport in Belfast")

Belfast is a relatively [car-dependent](/wiki/Car_dependency "Car dependency") city by European standards, with an extensive road network including the 22.5 miles (36 km) [M2](/wiki/M2_motorway_(Northern_Ireland) "M2 motorway (Northern Ireland)") and [M22 motorway](/wiki/M22_motorway_(Northern_Ireland) "M22 motorway (Northern Ireland)") route.[[317]](#cite_note-318) Reflecting this high volume of traffic, a 2025 study ranked Belfast as the 15th noisiest city in the United Kingdom.[[318]](#cite_note-319)

[Black taxis](/wiki/Hackney_carriage "Hackney carriage") are common in the city, operating on a [share](/wiki/Share_taxi "Share taxi") basis in some areas.[[319]](#cite_note-320) These are outnumbered by [private hire taxis](/wiki/Taxicabs_of_the_United_Kingdom#Private_hire_('minicabs') "Taxicabs of the United Kingdom"). Bus and rail public transport in Northern Ireland is operated by subsidiaries of [Translink](/wiki/Translink_(Northern_Ireland) "Translink (Northern Ireland)"). Bus services in the city proper and the nearer suburbs are operated by [Translink Metro](/wiki/Metro_(Belfast) "Metro (Belfast)"), with services focusing on linking residential districts with the city centre on 12 [quality bus corridors](/wiki/Quality_bus_corridor "Quality bus corridor") running along main radial roads,[[320]](#cite_note-321)

More distant suburbs are served by [Ulsterbus](/wiki/Ulsterbus "Ulsterbus"). [Northern Ireland Railways](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Railways "Northern Ireland Railways") provides [suburban services](/wiki/Belfast_Suburban_Rail "Belfast Suburban Rail") along three lines running through Belfast's northern suburbs to [Carrickfergus](/wiki/Carrickfergus "Carrickfergus"), [Larne](/wiki/Larne "Larne") and [Larne Harbour](/wiki/Larne_Harbour_railway_station "Larne Harbour railway station"), eastwards towards [Bangor](/wiki/Bangor,_County_Down "Bangor, County Down") and south-westwards towards [Lisburn](/wiki/Lisburn "Lisburn") and [Portadown](/wiki/Portadown_railway_station "Portadown railway station"). This service is known as the [Belfast Suburban Rail](/wiki/Belfast_Suburban_Rail "Belfast Suburban Rail") system. Belfast is linked [directly](/wiki/Belfast-Derry_railway_line "Belfast-Derry railway line") to [Coleraine](/wiki/Coleraine_railway_station "Coleraine railway station"), [Portrush](/wiki/Portrush_railway_station "Portrush railway station") and [Derry](/wiki/Derry_~_Londonderry_railway_station "Derry ~ Londonderry railway station"). Belfast has a direct rail connection with [Dublin](/wiki/Dublin "Dublin") called *[Enterprise](/wiki/Enterprise_(train) "Enterprise (train)")* operated jointly by NIR and the Irish rail company [Iarnród Éireann](/wiki/Iarnr%C3%B3d_%C3%89ireann "Iarnród Éireann").

In 2024, the city's Europa Bus Centre and Great Victoria Street rail station, was replaced by a new [Belfast Grand Central Station](/wiki/Belfast_Grand_Central_Station "Belfast Grand Central Station"). It is "the largest integrated transport facility on the island of Ireland" with bus stands, railway platforms, and facilities for taxis and bicycles.[[321]](#cite_note-322)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/George_Best_Belfast_City_Airport_-_geograph.org.uk_-_714574.jpg/250px-George_Best_Belfast_City_Airport_-_geograph.org.uk_-_714574.jpg)](/wiki/File:George_Best_Belfast_City_Airport_-_geograph.org.uk_-_714574.jpg)

[George Best Belfast City Airport](/wiki/George_Best_Belfast_City_Airport "George Best Belfast City Airport")

The city has two airports: [George Best Belfast City Airport](/wiki/George_Best_Belfast_City_Airport "George Best Belfast City Airport"), close to the city centre on the eastern shore of Belfast Lough and [Belfast International Airport](/wiki/Belfast_International_Airport "Belfast International Airport") 30–40 minutes to the west on the shore of [Lough Neagh](/wiki/Lough_Neagh "Lough Neagh"). Both operate UK domestic and European flights. The city is also served by [Dublin Airport](/wiki/Dublin_Airport "Dublin Airport"), two hours to the south, with direct inter-continental connections.

In addition to its extensive freight business, the Belfast Port offers car-ferry sailings, operated by [Stena Line](/wiki/Stena_Line "Stena Line"), to [Cairnryan](/wiki/Cairnryan "Cairnryan") in Scotland (5 Sailings Daily. 2 hours 22 minutes) and to [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool "Liverpool")-[Birkenhead](/wiki/Birkenhead "Birkenhead") (14 sailings weekly. 8 hours). The [Isle of Man Steam Packet](/wiki/Isle_of_Man_Steam_Packet "Isle of Man Steam Packet") Company provides a seasonal connection to [Douglas, Isle of Man](/wiki/Douglas,_Isle_of_Man "Douglas, Isle of Man").

The [Glider](/wiki/Glider_(Belfast) "Glider (Belfast)") bus service is a new form of transport in Belfast. Introduced in 2018, it is a [bus rapid transit](/wiki/Bus_rapid_transit "Bus rapid transit") system linking East Belfast, West Belfast and the Titanic Quarter from the City Centre.[[322]](#cite_note-323) Using [articulated buses](/wiki/Articulated_buses "Articulated buses"), the £90 million service saw a 17% increase in its first month in Belfast, with 30,000 more people using the Gliders every week. The service is being recognised as helping to modernise the city's public transport.[[323]](#cite_note-324)

[National Cycle Route 9](/wiki/National_Cycle_Route_9 "National Cycle Route 9") to [Newry](/wiki/Newry "Newry"),[[324]](#cite_note-325) which will eventually connect with Dublin,[[325]](#cite_note-326) starts in Belfast.

### Utilities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Utilities")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/SilentValley.jpg/250px-SilentValley.jpg)](/wiki/File:SilentValley.jpg)

Silent Valley Reservoir, showing the brick-built overflow

Half of Belfast's water is supplied via the [Aquarius pipeline](/wiki/Mourne_Conduit "Mourne Conduit") from the [Silent Valley Reservoir](/wiki/Silent_Valley_Reservoir "Silent Valley Reservoir") in County Down, created to collect water from the [Mourne Mountains](/wiki/Mourne_Mountains "Mourne Mountains").[[326]](#cite_note-327) The other half is now supplied from [Lough Neagh](/wiki/Lough_Neagh "Lough Neagh") via Dunore Water Treatment Works in County Antrim.[[327]](#cite_note-BMA_plan-328)[[328]](#cite_note-329) The citizens of Belfast pay for their water in their [rates](/wiki/Rates_(tax) "Rates (tax)") bill. Plans to bring in additional water tariffs were deferred by [devolution](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Assembly "Northern Ireland Assembly") in May 2007.[[329]](#cite_note-330)

Power is provided from a number of [power stations](/wiki/List_of_power_stations_in_Northern_Ireland "List of power stations in Northern Ireland") via [NIE Networks Limited](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Electricity "Northern Ireland Electricity") transmission lines. (Just under a half of electricity consumption in Northern Ireland is generated from [renewable sources](/wiki/Renewable_resource "Renewable resource")).[[330]](#cite_note-331) [Phoenix Natural Gas](/wiki/Phoenix_Natural_Gas "Phoenix Natural Gas") Ltd. started supplying customers in Larne and Greater Belfast with natural gas in 1996 via the newly constructed [Scotland-Northern Ireland pipeline](/wiki/Scotland-Northern_Ireland_pipeline "Scotland-Northern Ireland pipeline").[[327]](#cite_note-BMA_plan-328) [Rates](/wiki/Rates_(tax) "Rates (tax)") in Belfast (and the rest of Northern Ireland) were reformed in April 2007. The discrete [capital](/wiki/Capital_(economics) "Capital (economics)") value system means rates bills are determined by the capital value of each domestic property as assessed by the Valuation and Lands Agency.[[331]](#cite_note-332)

## Recreation and sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Recreation and sports")]

### Leisure centres

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Leisure centres")]

Belfast City Council owns and maintains 17 leisure centres across the city, run on its behalf by the non-profit [social enterprise](/wiki/Social_enterprise "Social enterprise") [GLL](/wiki/Greenwich_Leisure_Limited "Greenwich Leisure Limited") under the 'Better' brand.[[332]](#cite_note-333) These include eight large multipurposed centres complete with swimming pools: Ballysillan Leisure Centre and Grove Wellbeing Centre in North Belfast; the Andersonstown, Falls, Shankill and Whiterock leisure centres in West Belfast; Templemore Baths and Lisnasharragh Leisure Centre in East Belfast, and close to the city centre in South Belfast, the Olympia Leisure Centre and Spa,[[333]](#cite_note-334)

### Parks and gardens

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Parks and gardens")]

Main article: [List of parks and gardens in Belfast](/wiki/List_of_parks_and_gardens_in_Belfast "List of parks and gardens in Belfast")

Belfast has over [forty parks](/wiki/List_of_parks_and_gardens_in_Belfast "List of parks and gardens in Belfast"). The oldest (1828) and one of the most popular parks [Botanic Gardens](/wiki/Belfast_Botanic_Gardens "Belfast Botanic Gardens")[[334]](#cite_note-335) in the [Queen's Quarter](/wiki/Queen%27s_Quarter,_Belfast "Queen's Quarter, Belfast"). Built in the 1830s and designed by [Sir Charles Lanyon](/wiki/Charles_Lanyon "Charles Lanyon"), its Palm House is one of the earliest examples of a curvilinear and cast iron [glasshouse](/wiki/Greenhouse "Greenhouse").[[335]](#cite_note-336) Other attractions in the park include the recently restored Tropical Ravine, a humid jungle glen built in 1889,[[336]](#cite_note-337) rose gardens and public events ranging from live opera broadcasts to pop concerts.[[337]](#cite_note-338)

The largest municipal park in the city, and closest to the city centre, lies on the right bank of Lagan. The 100-acres of [Ormeau Park](/wiki/Ormeau_Park "Ormeau Park") were opened to the public in 1871 on what was the last demesne of the town's former proprietors, the Chichesters, [Marquesses of Donegall](/wiki/Marquess_of_Donegall "Marquess of Donegall").[[338]](#cite_note-339)

In north Belfast, the [Waterworks](/wiki/Water_Works,_Belfast "Water Works, Belfast"), two reservoirs to which the public have had access since 1897, are features of a park supporting angling and waterfowl.[[339]](#cite_note-340) In 1906, a further water park, [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria_Park,_Belfast "Victoria Park, Belfast"), opened behind industrial dockland on what had been the eastern shore of the Lough.[[340]](#cite_note-341) It is now connected through east Belfast by the Connswater Community Greenway which offers 16 km of continuous cycle and walkway through east Belfast.[[341]](#cite_note-342)

The largest green conservation area within the city's boundaries is a 2,116 hectares patchwork of "parks, demesnes, woodland and meadows" stretching upriver along the banks of the Lagan river and canal;[[115]](#cite_note-:12-116) Established in 1967, the Lagan Valley Regional Park envelopes in its course, Belvoir Park Forest, which contains ancient oaks and a 12th-century Norman [Motte](/wiki/Motte-and-bailey_castle "Motte-and-bailey castle"),[[342]](#cite_note-343) and [Sir Thomas and Lady Dixon Park](/wiki/Sir_Thomas_and_Lady_Dixon_Park "Sir Thomas and Lady Dixon Park"), whose International Rose Garden attracts thousands of visitors each July.[[343]](#cite_note-Go_To_Belfast:_Sir_Thomas_and_Lady_Dixon_Park-344)

Colin Glenn Forest Park,[[344]](#cite_note-345) the [National Trust](/wiki/National_Trust "National Trust") Divis and the Black Mountain Ridge Trail,[[345]](#cite_note-:14-346) and Cave Hill Country Park.[[346]](#cite_note-347) offer panoramic views over Belfast and beyond from the west.[[345]](#cite_note-:14-346) Climbing the Castlereagh Hills, the National Trust Lisnabreeny Cregagh Glen does the same from the east.[[347]](#cite_note-348)

Below Cave Hill, the council maintains one of the few local government-funded zoos in the British Isles. The [Belfast Zoo](/wiki/Belfast_Zoo "Belfast Zoo") houses more than 1,200 animals of 140 species including [Asian elephants](/wiki/Asian_elephant "Asian elephant"), [Barbary lions](/wiki/Barbary_lion "Barbary lion"), [Malayan sun bears](/wiki/Malayan_sun_bear "Malayan sun bear") (one of the few in the United Kingdom), two species of penguin, a family of [western lowland gorillas](/wiki/Western_lowland_gorilla "Western lowland gorilla"), a troop of [common chimpanzees](/wiki/Common_chimpanzee "Common chimpanzee"), a pair of [red pandas](/wiki/Red_panda "Red panda"), a pair of [Goodfellow's tree-kangaroos](/wiki/Goodfellow%27s_tree-kangaroo "Goodfellow's tree-kangaroo") and [Francois' langurs](/wiki/Francois%27_langur "Francois' langur"). It carries out important conservation work and takes part in European and international breeding programmes which help to ensure the survival of many species under threat.[[348]](#cite_note-349)

### Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Sports")]

Main article: [Sport in Belfast](/wiki/Sport_in_Belfast "Sport in Belfast")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/Windsor_Park_redevelopment_.jpg/250px-Windsor_Park_redevelopment_.jpg)](/wiki/File:Windsor_Park_redevelopment_.jpg)

[Irish Football Association](/wiki/Irish_Football_Association "Irish Football Association") stadium Windsor Park

Belfast has several notable sports teams playing a diverse variety of sports such as [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football"), [Gaelic games](/wiki/Gaelic_games "Gaelic games"), [rugby](/wiki/Rugby_football "Rugby football"), [cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket"), and [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey"). The [Belfast Marathon](/wiki/Belfast_Marathon "Belfast Marathon") is run annually on May Day, The 41st Marathon in 2023, with related events (Wheelchair Race, Team Relay and 8 Mile Walk) attracted 15,000 participants.[[349]](#cite_note-350)

The [Northern Ireland national football team](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_national_football_team "Northern Ireland national football team") plays its home matches at [Windsor Park](/wiki/Windsor_Park "Windsor Park"). Football clubs with stadia and training grounds in the city include: [Linfield](/wiki/Linfield_F.C. "Linfield F.C."), [Glentoran](/wiki/Glentoran_F.C. "Glentoran F.C."), [Crusaders](/wiki/Crusaders_F.C. "Crusaders F.C."), [Cliftonville](/wiki/Cliftonville_F.C. "Cliftonville F.C."), [Donegal Celtic](/wiki/Donegal_Celtic "Donegal Celtic"), [Harland & Wolff Welders](/wiki/Harland_%26_Wolff_Welders_F.C. "Harland & Wolff Welders F.C."), [Dundela](/wiki/Dundela_F.C. "Dundela F.C."), [Knockbreda](/wiki/Knockbreda_F.C. "Knockbreda F.C."), [PSNI](/wiki/PSNI_F.C. "PSNI F.C."), [Newington](/wiki/Newington_Youth_F.C. "Newington Youth F.C."), [Sport & Leisure](/wiki/Sport_%26_Leisure_Swifts_F.C. "Sport & Leisure Swifts F.C.") and [Brantwood](/wiki/Brantwood_F.C. "Brantwood F.C.").[[350]](#cite_note-351)

Belfast is home to over twenty [Gaelic football](/wiki/Gaelic_football "Gaelic football") and [hurling](/wiki/Hurling "Hurling") clubs.[[351]](#cite_note-352) [Casement Park](/wiki/Casement_Park "Casement Park") in west Belfast, home to the [Antrim](/wiki/Antrim_GAA "Antrim GAA") county teams, had a capacity of 31,500 making it the second largest [Gaelic Athletic Association](/wiki/Gaelic_Athletic_Association "Gaelic Athletic Association") ground in [Ulster](/wiki/Ulster "Ulster").[[352]](#cite_note-353) Listed as one of the venues for the UK and Ireland's successful [UEFA Euro 2028](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2028 "UEFA Euro 2028") bid, with co-funding from the Irish government there are plans for a complete rebuild.[[353]](#cite_note-354) In May 2020, the foundation of [East Belfast GAA](/wiki/East_Belfast_GAA "East Belfast GAA") returned Gaelic Games to East Belfast after decades of its absence in the area. The current club president is Irish-language enthusiast [Linda Ervine](/wiki/Linda_Ervine "Linda Ervine") who comes from a unionist background in the area. The team currently plays in the Down Senior County League.[[354]](#cite_note-355)

The 1999 [Heineken Cup](/wiki/Heineken_Cup "Heineken Cup") champions [Ulster Rugby](/wiki/Ulster_Rugby "Ulster Rugby") play at [Ravenhill Stadium](/wiki/Ravenhill_Stadium "Ravenhill Stadium") in the south of the city. Belfast has four teams in rugby's [All-Ireland League](/wiki/All-Ireland_League_(rugby_union) "All-Ireland League (rugby union)"): [Belfast Harlequins](/wiki/Belfast_Harlequins "Belfast Harlequins") in Division 1B; and [Instonians](/wiki/Instonians "Instonians"), [Queen's University](/wiki/Queen%27s_University_RFC "Queen's University RFC") and [Malone](/wiki/Malone_RFC "Malone RFC") in Division 2A.

Belfast is home to the [Stormont cricket ground](/wiki/Stormont_(cricket_ground) "Stormont (cricket ground)") since 1949 and was the venue for the [Irish cricket team](/wiki/Irish_cricket_team "Irish cricket team")'s first ever [One Day International](/wiki/One_Day_International "One Day International") against [England](/wiki/England_cricket_team "England cricket team") in 2006.[[355]](#cite_note-356)

The 9,500 capacity [SSE Arena](/wiki/SSE_Arena,_Belfast "SSE Arena, Belfast") accommodates the [Belfast Giants](/wiki/Belfast_Giants "Belfast Giants"), one of the biggest [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey") clubs in the UK. Featuring Canadian, ex-[NHL](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League") players, the club competes the British [Elite Ice Hockey League](/wiki/Elite_Ice_Hockey_League "Elite Ice Hockey League").

Belfast was the home town of former [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.") player [George Best](/wiki/George_Best "George Best"), the 1968 [European Footballer of the Year](/wiki/European_Footballer_of_the_Year "European Footballer of the Year"), who died in November 2005. On the day he was buried in the city, 100,000 people lined the route from his home on the Cregagh Road to Roselawn cemetery.[[356]](#cite_note-357) Since his death the [City Airport](/wiki/George_Best_Belfast_City_Airport "George Best Belfast City Airport") was named after him and a trust has been set up to fund a memorial to him in the city centre.[[357]](#cite_note-358) Other sportspeople celebrated in the city include double world snooker champion [Alex "Hurricane" Higgins](/wiki/Alex_Higgins "Alex Higgins")[[358]](#cite_note-359) and world champion boxers [Wayne McCullough](/wiki/Wayne_McCullough "Wayne McCullough"), [Rinty Monaghan](/wiki/Rinty_Monaghan "Rinty Monaghan") and [Carl Frampton](/wiki/Carl_Frampton "Carl Frampton").[[359]](#cite_note-360)

## Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Climate")]

Main article: [Climate of Belfast](/wiki/Climate_of_Belfast "Climate of Belfast")

At [54°35′49″N 05°55′45″W﻿ / ﻿54.59694°N 5.92917°W﻿ / 54.59694; -5.92917](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Belfast&params=54_35_49_N_05_55_45_W_), its northern latitude is characterised by short winter days and long summer evenings. During the [winter solstice](/wiki/Winter_solstice "Winter solstice"), the shortest day of the year, local sunset is before 16:00 while sunrise is around 08:45. At the [summer solstice](/wiki/Solstice "Solstice") in June, the sun sets after 22:00 and rises before 05:00.[[360]](#cite_note-361)

For this northern latitude, thanks to the influence of the [Gulf Stream](/wiki/Gulf_Stream "Gulf Stream") and [North Atlantic Drift](/wiki/North_Atlantic_Current "North Atlantic Current"), Belfast has a comparatively mild climate. In summer the temperatures rarely range above 25 °C (77 °F) or dip in winter below −5 °C (23 °F).[[361]](#cite_note-362)[[362]](#cite_note-363) The maritime influence, also ensures that the city gets significant precipitation. On 157 days in an average year, rainfall is greater than 1 mm. Average annual rainfall is 846 mm (33.3 in),[[363]](#cite_note-Belfast_Weather2-364) less than areas of northern England or most of [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland"),[[364]](#cite_note-Met_Office2-365) but higher than [Dublin](/wiki/Dublin "Dublin") or the south-east coast of Ireland.[[365]](#cite_note-366)

With its moderate temperatures and abundant rainfall, Belfast's climate is defined as a [temperate](/wiki/Temperate "Temperate") [oceanic climate](/wiki/Oceanic_climate "Oceanic climate") (Cfb in the [Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") system), a classification it shares with most of northwest Europe.[[366]](#cite_note-367)

* [Brian Moore](/wiki/Brian_Moore_(novelist) "Brian Moore (novelist)"), *[The Emperor of Ice Cream](/wiki/The_Emperor_of_Ice-Cream_(novel) "The Emperor of Ice-Cream (novel)")* (1965).
* [Robert McLiam Wilson](/wiki/Robert_McLiam_Wilson "Robert McLiam Wilson"), [*Eureka Street*](/wiki/Eureka_Street_(novel) "Eureka Street (novel)") (1996)
* *['71](/wiki/%2771_(film) "'71 (film)")*, a British [thriller film](/wiki/Thriller_film "Thriller film") directed by [Yann Demange](/wiki/Yann_Demange "Yann Demange"), starring [Jack O'Connell](/wiki/Jack_O%27Connell_(actor) "Jack O'Connell (actor)") (2014)

## Notable people

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Notable people")]

See also: [List of people from Belfast](/wiki/List_of_people_from_Belfast "List of people from Belfast")

### Georgian Belfast

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: Georgian Belfast")]

* [Edward Bunting](/wiki/Edward_Bunting "Edward Bunting") (1773–1843), Irish folklorist, organiser of the 1792 [Belfast Harp Festival](/wiki/Belfast_Harp_Festival "Belfast Harp Festival")
* [Henry Cooke](/wiki/Henry_Cooke_(minister) "Henry Cooke (minister)") (1788–1868), [Presbyterian Moderator](/wiki/List_of_Moderators_of_the_Presbyterian_Church_in_Ireland "List of Moderators of the Presbyterian Church in Ireland"), evangelist, proponent of "Protestant unity" (Cooke Memorial Church, May Street, "Black Man" statue College Square East)
* [Waddell Cunningham](/wiki/Waddell_Cunningham "Waddell Cunningham") (1729–1797), Trans-Atlantic trader, [West-Indian slaveholder](/wiki/West_Indies "West Indies"), [Irish Volunteer](/wiki/Irish_Volunteers_(18th_century) "Irish Volunteers (18th century)"), liberal patron
* [William Drennan](/wiki/William_Drennan "William Drennan") (1754–1820), [United Irishman](/wiki/Society_of_United_Irishmen "Society of United Irishmen"), founder of the [Royal Belfast Academical Institution](/wiki/Royal_Belfast_Academical_Institution "Royal Belfast Academical Institution") (RBAI)
* [Mary Ann McCracken](/wiki/Mary_Ann_McCracken "Mary Ann McCracken") (1766–1866), [United Irishwoman](/wiki/Society_of_United_Irishmen "Society of United Irishmen"), social activist, [abolitionist](/wiki/Abolitionism "Abolitionism"), sister of [Henry Joy McCracken](/wiki/Henry_Joy_McCracken "Henry Joy McCracken") hanged 1798. (Statue at City Hall).
* [James MacDonnell](/wiki/James_MacDonnell_(physician) "James MacDonnell (physician)") (1763–1845), physician, [polymath](/wiki/Polymath "Polymath") patron of institutions since developed as the [Royal Victoria Hospital](/wiki/Royal_Victoria_Hospital,_Belfast "Royal Victoria Hospital, Belfast"), [RBAI](/wiki/Royal_Belfast_Academical_Institution "Royal Belfast Academical Institution") and the [Linen Hall Library](/wiki/Linen_Hall_Library "Linen Hall Library")
* [Martha McTier](/wiki/Martha_McTier "Martha McTier") (1742–1837), [United Irishwoman](/wiki/Society_of_United_Irishmen "Society of United Irishmen"), advocate for women's health and education
* [David Manson](/wiki/David_Manson_(schoolmaster) "David Manson (schoolmaster)"), (1726–1792), schoolmaster, pioneer of play and peer tutoring. Freedom of the Borough 1779
* [Samuel Neilson](/wiki/Samuel_Neilson "Samuel Neilson") (1761–1803), woollen merchant, publisher of the [Northern Star](/wiki/Northern_Star_(newspaper_of_the_Society_of_United_Irishmen) "Northern Star (newspaper of the Society of United Irishmen)"), [United Irishman](/wiki/Society_of_United_Irishmen "Society of United Irishmen")
* [John Templeton](/wiki/John_Templeton_(Botanist) "John Templeton (Botanist)") (1766–1825), "Father of Irish Botany", patron of the town's scientific and literary societies

### Victorian Belfast

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=51 "Edit section: Victorian Belfast")]

* [Thomas Andrews](/wiki/Thomas_Andrews "Thomas Andrews") (1873–1912), chief naval architect at Harland & Wolff, went down with [RMS *Titanic*](/wiki/RMS_Titanic "RMS Titanic")
* [Joseph Biggar](/wiki/Joseph_Biggar "Joseph Biggar") (1828–1890),["obstructionist"](/wiki/Obstructionism "Obstructionism") [Irish nationalist](/wiki/Irish_Parliamentary_Party "Irish Parliamentary Party") [MP](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament_(United_Kingdom) "Member of Parliament (United Kingdom)"), women's suffragist
* [Margaret Byers](/wiki/Margaret_Byers "Margaret Byers") (1832–1912), educator, activist, social reformer, missionary, founder of [Victoria College](/wiki/Victoria_College,_Belfast "Victoria College, Belfast")
* [Hugh "Roaring" Hanna](/wiki/Hugh_Hanna "Hugh Hanna") (1821–1892), Protestant evangelist associated with sectarian riot (Commemorated, until targeted and destroyed in [the Troubles](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Troubles "Northern Ireland Troubles"), by his statue and church at Carlisle Circus).
* [Edward Harland](/wiki/Edward_Harland "Edward Harland") (1831–1895) and [Gustave Wolff](/wiki/Gustav_Wilhelm_Wolff "Gustav Wilhelm Wolff") (1834–1913), partners in the [world's largest shipyard](/wiki/Harland_%26_Wolff "Harland & Wolff"). Statue at City Hall
* [Bernard "Barney" Hughes](/wiki/Bernard_Hughes "Bernard Hughes") (1808–1878), Ireland's largest miller and baker (producer of the [Belfast bap](/wiki/Belfast_bap "Belfast bap")), first elected Catholic town councillor
* [Otto Jaffe](/wiki/Otto_Jaffe "Otto Jaffe") (1846–1929), business, and Jewish community, leader, twice [Mayor of Belfast](/wiki/Mayor_of_Belfast "Mayor of Belfast")
* [William Johnston](/wiki/William_Johnston_(Irish_politician) "William Johnston (Irish politician)") (1829–1902), Orangeman celebrated for breaking the [Party Processions Act](/wiki/Party_Processions_Act_1850 "Party Processions Act 1850"), Belfast MP, women's suffragist
* [Richard Rutledge Kane](/wiki/Richard_Rutledge_Kane_(senior) "Richard Rutledge Kane (senior)") (1841–1898), [Orange Order](/wiki/Orange_Order "Orange Order") Grand Master, patron of the first Belfast branch of the [Gaelic League](/wiki/Gaelic_League "Gaelic League")
* [Charles Lanyon](/wiki/Charles_Lanyon "Charles Lanyon") (1813–1889), architect of main ("Lanyon") building of [Queens University](/wiki/Queens_University,_Belfast "Queens University, Belfast"), the [Palm House, Botanic Gardens](/wiki/Botanic_Gardens_(Belfast) "Botanic Gardens (Belfast)"), [Linenhall Library](/wiki/Linenhall_Library "Linenhall Library"), [Belfast Castle](/wiki/Belfast_Castle "Belfast Castle") and [Crumlin Road Goal](/wiki/HM_Prison_Belfast "HM Prison Belfast") and [Courthouse](/wiki/Crumlin_Road_Courthouse "Crumlin Road Courthouse")
* [Robert Shipboy MacAdam](/wiki/Robert_Shipboy_MacAdam "Robert Shipboy MacAdam") (1808–1895), Irish folklorist and linguist, honoured with [Cardinal Ó Fiaich](/wiki/Tom%C3%A1s_%C3%93_Fiaich "Tomás Ó Fiaich") in [Cultúrlann McAdam Ó Fiaich](/wiki/Cult%C3%BArlann_McAdam_%C3%93_Fiaich "Cultúrlann McAdam Ó Fiaich")
* [John Mulholland](/wiki/John_Mulholland,_1st_Baron_Dunleath "John Mulholland, 1st Baron Dunleath") (1819–1895), established the world's largest flax-spinning operation, York Street Mill; MP
* [William Pirrie](/wiki/William_Pirrie,_1st_Viscount_Pirrie "William Pirrie, 1st Viscount Pirrie") (1847–1924), Chairman of [Harland & Wolff](/wiki/Harland_%26_Wolff "Harland & Wolff"), Mayor of Belfast; Freedom of the city, 1898. (Statue at City Hall).
* [William Thomson, Lord Kelvin](/wiki/William_Thomson,_1st_Baron_Kelvin "William Thomson, 1st Baron Kelvin") (1824–1907), physicist renowned for his work on mechanical energy and heat; memorial statue stands before the [Ulster Museum](/wiki/Ulster_Museum "Ulster Museum")
* [Isabella Tod](/wiki/Isabella_Tod "Isabella Tod") (1836–1896), suffragist, with [William Johnston](/wiki/William_Johnston_(Irish_politician) "William Johnston (Irish politician)") secured the municipal vote for women, 1888

### Early 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=52 "Edit section: Early 20th century")]

* [Winifred Carney](/wiki/Winifred_Carney "Winifred Carney") (1887–1943), [suffragist](/wiki/Women%27s_suffrage "Women's suffrage"), [rebel 1916](/wiki/Easter_Rising "Easter Rising"), labour activist. (Statue at City Hall).
* [Thomas Carnduff](/wiki/Thomas_Carnduff "Thomas Carnduff") (1886–1956), shipyard poet, playwright, trade unionist, [Independent Orangeman](/wiki/Independent_Orange_Order "Independent Orange Order").
* [Edward Carson](/wiki/Edward_Carson "Edward Carson") (1854–1935), leader of [Ulster Unionism](/wiki/Unionism_in_Ireland "Unionism in Ireland") in the [Home Rule Crisis](/wiki/Home_Rule_Crisis "Home Rule Crisis"). (Statue before the [Parliament Buildings at Stormont](/wiki/Parliament_Buildings_(Northern_Ireland) "Parliament Buildings (Northern Ireland)"))
* [William Conor](/wiki/William_Conor "William Conor") (1881–1968), painter renowned for his sympathetic portrayals of working-class life
* [William Conway](/wiki/William_Conway_(cardinal) "William Conway (cardinal)") (1913–1977), [Cardinal](/wiki/Cardinal_(Catholic_Church) "Cardinal (Catholic Church)") and [All--Ireland Primate](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Armagh "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Armagh"), co-founder of [Trócaire](/wiki/Tr%C3%B3caire "Trócaire")
* [James Craig](/wiki/James_Craig,_1st_Viscount_Craigavon "James Craig, 1st Viscount Craigavon") (1871–1940), [Ulster Unionist](/wiki/Ulster_Unionist_Party "Ulster Unionist Party"), first [Prime Minister of Northern Ireland](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Northern_Ireland "Prime Minister of Northern Ireland")
* [Joseph Devlin](/wiki/Joseph_Devlin "Joseph Devlin") (1871–1934), journalist, [Irish nationalist](/wiki/Irish_Parliamentary_Party "Irish Parliamentary Party") [Westminster](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom") and [Stormont](/wiki/Parliament_of_Northern_Ireland "Parliament of Northern Ireland") MP, President of the [Ancient Order of Hibernians](/wiki/Ancient_Order_of_Hibernians "Ancient Order of Hibernians")
* [Harry Ferguson](/wiki/Harry_Ferguson "Harry Ferguson") (1884–1960), developer of the modern agricultural tractor, first person in Ireland to build and fly an aeroplane
* [John Hewitt](/wiki/John_Hewitt_(poet) "John Hewitt (poet)") (1907–1987), poet ("The Bloody Brae"). Freedom of the City 1983
* [C. S. Lewis](/wiki/C._S._Lewis "C. S. Lewis") (1898–1963), writer and [Anglican](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism") [lay theologian](/wiki/Lay_theologian "Lay theologian"). (Honoured as author of [The Chronicles of Narnia](/wiki/The_Chronicles_of_Narnia "The Chronicles of Narnia") in C. S. Lewis Square, East Belfast).
* [Margaret McCoubrey](/wiki/Margaret_McCoubrey "Margaret McCoubrey") (1880–1955), militant [WPSU](/wiki/Women%27s_Social_and_Political_Union "Women's Social and Political Union") suffragette, peace campaigner, [Labour](/wiki/Belfast_Labour_Party "Belfast Labour Party") City Councillor
* [Harry Midgley](/wiki/Harry_Midgley "Harry Midgley") (1893–1957), labour union and [party](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Labour_Party "Northern Ireland Labour Party") organiser, post-war [Unionist](/wiki/Ulster_Unionist_Party "Ulster Unionist Party") Minister for Education
* [Cathal O'Byrne](/wiki/Cathal_O%27Byrne "Cathal O'Byrne") (1876–1957), writer, actor, journalist.
* [Alexander "Buck Alec" Robinson](/wiki/Alexander_Robinson "Alexander Robinson") (1901–1995), docklands streetfighter and loyalist gunman. Kept lions in his [Sailortown](/wiki/Sailortown_(Belfast) "Sailortown (Belfast)") home
* [Betty Sinclair](/wiki/Betty_Sinclair "Betty Sinclair") (1910–1981), [Communist party](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Ireland "Communist Party of Ireland") activist, 1932 Outdoor Relief protest, [Northern Ireland Civil Rights Association](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Civil_Rights_Association "Northern Ireland Civil Rights Association") chair
* [Joseph Tomelty](/wiki/Joseph_Tomelty "Joseph Tomelty") (1911–1995), stage and screen ([Odd Man Out](/wiki/Odd_Man_Out "Odd Man Out")) actor, writer and broadcaster
* [William Walker](/wiki/William_Walker_(trade_unionist) "William Walker (trade unionist)") (1871–1918), unionist labour organiser and vice-chair of the [British Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)")
* [Ernest Walton](/wiki/Ernest_Walton "Ernest Walton") (1903–95), with [John Cockcroft](/wiki/John_Cockcroft "John Cockcroft") Nobel Prize for splitting the atom

### Late 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=53 "Edit section: Late 20th century")]

* [Gerry Adams](/wiki/Gerry_Adams "Gerry Adams") (born 1948), reputed republican paramilitary ([PIRA](/wiki/Provisional_Irish_Republican_Army "Provisional Irish Republican Army")) leader, president of [Sinn Féin](/wiki/Sinn_F%C3%A9in "Sinn Féin"); MP Belfast West. rtd.
* [William M. Anderson](/wiki/William_M._Anderson "William M. Anderson") (1948–2022), film editor
* [Derek Bell](/wiki/Derek_Bell_(musician) "Derek Bell (musician)") (1935–2002), harpist, musicologist and composer, [The Chieftains](/wiki/The_Chieftains "The Chieftains")
* [George Best](/wiki/George_Best "George Best") (1946–2005), football international, iconic sports figure, [City Airport](/wiki/George_Best_Belfast_City_Airport "George Best Belfast City Airport") named in his honour
* [May Blood](/wiki/May_Blood,_Baroness_Blood "May Blood, Baroness Blood") (1938–2022), [shop steward](/wiki/Union_representative "Union representative") in one of the city's last linen mills, community worker, co-founder [Northern Ireland Women's Coalition](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Women%27s_Coalition "Northern Ireland Women's Coalition")
* [John Boyd](/wiki/John_Boyd_(playwright) "John Boyd (playwright)") (1912–2002), playwright, broadcaster
* [Ciaran Carson](/wiki/Ciaran_Carson "Ciaran Carson") (1948–2019), writer, poet ([Belfast Confetti](/wiki/Belfast_Confetti_(poem) "Belfast Confetti (poem)"))
* [George Cassidy](/wiki/George_Cassidy_(jazz_musician) "George Cassidy (jazz musician)") (1936–2023), jazz musician and music teacher
* [Mairéad Corrigan](/wiki/Mairead_Maguire "Mairead Maguire") (born 1944), with [Betty Williams](/wiki/Betty_Williams "Betty Williams") awarded the 1976 [Nobel Peace Prize](/wiki/Nobel_Peace_Prize "Nobel Peace Prize"), as co-founder of Women for Peace / the Peace People, critic of US and UK foreign policy
* [David Ervine](/wiki/David_Ervine "David Ervine") (1953–2007), loyalist paramilitary ([UVF](/wiki/Ulster_Volunteer_Force "Ulster Volunteer Force")) veteran, leader of the pro-[Agreement](/wiki/Good_Friday_Agreement "Good Friday Agreement") [Progressive Unionist Party](/wiki/Progressive_Unionist_Party "Progressive Unionist Party"); MLA
* [Gerry Fitt](/wiki/Gerry_Fitt "Gerry Fitt") (1926–2005), [Republican Labour](/wiki/Republican_Labour_Party "Republican Labour Party")/[SDLP](/wiki/Social_Democratic_and_Labour_Party "Social Democratic and Labour Party") MP. Deputy Chief of the first NI [power-sharing](/wiki/Power-sharing "Power-sharing") [executive, 1974](/wiki/Executive_of_the_1974_Northern_Ireland_Assembly "Executive of the 1974 Northern Ireland Assembly")
* [David Hammond](/wiki/David_Hammond_(broadcaster) "David Hammond (broadcaster)") (1928–2008), teacher, singer, broadcaster, and film-maker who documented the culture of the city's shipyards and streets
* [Terri Hooley](/wiki/Terri_Hooley "Terri Hooley") (born 1948), key figure in the Belfast punk scene, celebrated in the 2013 biopic [Good Vibrations](/wiki/Good_Vibrations_(film) "Good Vibrations (film)")
* [Brian Keenan](/wiki/Brian_Keenan_(Irish_republican) "Brian Keenan (Irish republican)") (1942–2008), directed [PIRA](/wiki/Provisional_Irish_Republican_Army "Provisional Irish Republican Army") bombing in the city, interlocutor for [arms decommissioning](/wiki/Decommissioning_in_Northern_Ireland "Decommissioning in Northern Ireland")
* [Helen Lewis](/wiki/Helen_Lewis_(choreographer) "Helen Lewis (choreographer)") (née Katz; 1916–2009), [Holocaust](/wiki/Holocaust "Holocaust") survivor, teacher and choreographer, pioneer in Northern Ireland of modern dance
* [Brian Moore](/wiki/Brian_Moore_(novelist) "Brian Moore (novelist)") (1921–1999), novelist ([The Lonely Passion of Judith Hearne](/wiki/Judith_Hearne "Judith Hearne"), [The Emperor of Ice Cream](/wiki/The_Emperor_of_Ice-Cream_(novel) "The Emperor of Ice-Cream (novel)"))
* [Van Morrison](/wiki/Van_Morrison "Van Morrison") (born 1945), singer-songwriter and musician
* [Ian Paisley](/wiki/Ian_Paisley "Ian Paisley") (1926–2014), Protestant evangelist ([Martyrs Memorial Church, Belfast](/wiki/Free_Presbyterian_Church_of_Ulster "Free Presbyterian Church of Ulster")), founder of the [DUP](/wiki/Democratic_Unionist_Party "Democratic Unionist Party"), [NI First Minister](/wiki/First_Minister_of_Northern_Ireland "First Minister of Northern Ireland")
* [Saidie Patterson](/wiki/Saidie_Patterson "Saidie Patterson") (1906–1985), feminist, trade unionist, peace activist.
* Fr [Alec Reid](/wiki/Alec_Reid "Alec Reid") (1931–2013), Catholic priest, mediator in the [Hume–Adams talks](/wiki/Hume%E2%80%93Adams_dialogue "Hume–Adams dialogue"), [Northern Ireland peace process](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_peace_process "Northern Ireland peace process")
* [David Trimble](/wiki/David_Trimble,_Baron_Trimble "David Trimble, Baron Trimble") (1944–2022), [Ulster Unionist](/wiki/Ulster_Unionist_Party "Ulster Unionist Party") leader, [Nobel Peace Prize](/wiki/Nobel_Peace_Prize "Nobel Peace Prize") laureate, [NI First Minister](/wiki/First_Minister_of_Northern_Ireland "First Minister of Northern Ireland")
* [Andy Tyrie](/wiki/Andy_Tyrie "Andy Tyrie") (1940–2025), loyalist paramilitary ([UDA](/wiki/Ulster_Defence_Association "Ulster Defence Association")) leader (rtd), [Ulster Workers' Council strike](/wiki/Ulster_Workers%27_Council_strike "Ulster Workers' Council strike"), [NI peace process](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_peace_process "Northern Ireland peace process") advocate
* Fr. [Des Wilson](/wiki/Des_Wilson_(Irish_Catholic_priest) "Des Wilson (Irish Catholic priest)") (1925–2019), [dissident Catholic priest](/wiki/Liberation_theology "Liberation theology"), west Belfast community activist, republican-loyalist mediator

## Twin towns – sister cities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=54 "Edit section: Twin towns – sister cities")]

Belfast City Council takes part in the [twinning scheme](/wiki/Twin_towns_and_sister_cities "Twin towns and sister cities"),[[370]](#cite_note-2014_cities-372) and is twinned with the following sister cities:

* [Nashville](/wiki/Nashville "Nashville"), [Tennessee](/wiki/Tennessee "Tennessee"), United States (since 1994)
* [Hefei](/wiki/Hefei "Hefei"), Anhui, China (since 2005)
* [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston"), Massachusetts, United States (since 2014)
* [Shenyang](/wiki/Shenyang "Shenyang"), Liaoning, China (since 2016)[[371]](#cite_note-373)
* [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne "Melbourne"), [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria_(state) "Victoria (state)"), Australia

## Freedom of the City

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Belfast&action=edit&section=55 "Edit section: Freedom of the City")]

Those who have received the [Freedom of the City](/wiki/Freedom_of_the_City "Freedom of the City"):[[372]](#cite_note-:10-374)

* Sir [Kenneth Branagh](/wiki/Kenneth_Branagh "Kenneth Branagh"): 30 January 2018.
* [Andrew Carnegie](/wiki/Andrew_Carnegie "Andrew Carnegie"): 28 September 1910.
* Sir [Winston Churchill](/wiki/Winston_Churchill "Winston Churchill"): 16 December 1955.[[373]](#cite_note-375)
* [Bill Clinton](/wiki/Bill_Clinton "Bill Clinton"), 9 April 2018
* [Sir Robert Hart](/wiki/Sir_Robert_Hart,_1st_Baronet "Sir Robert Hart, 1st Baronet"), 1 July 1908
* [John Hewitt](/wiki/John_Hewitt_(poet) "John Hewitt (poet)"): 26 May 1983
* [Sir John Jordan](/wiki/John_Jordan_(diplomat) "John Jordan (diplomat)"): 28 September 1910.
* [Michael Longley](/wiki/Michael_Longley "Michael Longley"): 23 March 2015
* [George J. Mitchell](/wiki/George_J._Mitchell "George J. Mitchell"), 9 April 2018
* Nurses of Belfast, 1 December 2015
* [Royal Ulster Constabulary](/wiki/Royal_Ulster_Constabulary "Royal Ulster Constabulary") and Reserve: 30 May 1980
* [William Pirrie, 1st Viscount Pirrie](/wiki/William_Pirrie,_1st_Viscount_Pirrie "William Pirrie, 1st Viscount Pirrie"): 1898, the first person to be awarded Freedom Of The City of Belfast.
